The default is FALSE to preserve the pre-v0.16 behavior where each sub-plot sizes its columns to its own content.
Since v0.19 the sidebar also has an Align column widths checkbox in its footer — users can toggle this behavior at runtime without going back to R.

title: Split Plots
text:
Sidebar Features
The sidebar includes:

Section headers — split-variable names displayed as uppercase labels
Collapsible — click the chevron to collapse the sidebar
Search — filter subgroups by typing
Keyboard navigation — arrow keys move between subgroups
Bold active — currently selected subgroup is highlighted

title: Split Plots
Split Plots
text:
Exporting Split Plots
Pass the SplitForest to save_split_table() (or save_plot(), which dispatches automatically). The directory structure mirrors the split hierarchy:
p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

save_split_table(p, "output", format = "svg")
# Writes: output/Male/Male_Young.svg, output/Female/Female_Old.svg, ...
See Export for save_plot() / save_split_table() details.

title: Shiny Integration
text: tabviz works seamlessly with Shiny through the htmlwidgets framework. This page covers basic integration, the proxy API for live updates, and split-table outputs.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Output and Render








UI
Server
Used for




tabvizOutput()
renderTabviz()
A single tabviz widget


splitTabvizOutput()
renderSplitTabviz()
A SplitForest (tabviz + sidebar of subgroups)



library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Forest Plot Explorer"),
  tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(meta_data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

Sizing
Both output functions accept standard CSS sizing:



Argument
Default
Examples




width
"100%"
"800px", "50vw"


height
"400px" ("600px" for split)
"600px", "80vh"

title: Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
text:
The Proxy API
Proxy functions update the plot without a full re-render — scroll position, selection state, and layout stay stable.







Function
Purpose




tabviz_proxy(id)
Reference a tabvizOutput for live updates


split_tabviz_proxy(id)
Reference a splitTabvizOutput for live updates


split_tabviz_select(proxy, key)
Switch a split table to a specific subgroup









ImportantAlways create proxies inside reactive contexts



Never create a proxy at the top level of your app. Both tabviz_proxy() and split_tabviz_proxy() require an active Shiny reactive domain.



Fluent verbs dispatch on proxies
Every fluent modifier — every set_*, add_*, remove_*, sort_*, filter_*, plus set_cell() and clear_edits() — dispatches on tabviz_proxy and sends a custom message to the running widget instead of mutating a static spec. The same call site works on a WebSpec, an htmlwidget, and a proxy:







Category
Verbs




Columns
add_column(), remove_column(), move_column(), resize_column(), update_column()


Rows
sort_rows(), filter_rows(), clear_filters(), select_rows(), move_row()


Data
update_data() (proxy requires a fresh WebSpec)


Groups
toggle_group()


Cells
set_cell(), set_row_label(), clear_edits() (proxy-only)


Global
set_theme(), set_zoom()





Sorting and filtering
observeEvent(input$sort_asc,  sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", "asc"))
observeEvent(input$sort_desc, sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", "desc"))

observeEvent(input$filter_n, {
  filter_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "n", "gt", input$min_sample_size)
})

observeEvent(input$clear_filters, {
  clear_filters(tabviz_proxy("plot"))
})
Filter operators: "eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains".


Replacing the dataset
update_data() on a proxy takes a WebSpec, not a raw data frame. Build one with tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE):
observeEvent(input$switch_dataset, {
  new_spec <- tabviz(
    get_dataset(input$dataset_name),
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ),
    .spec_only = TRUE
  )
  update_data(tabviz_proxy("plot"), new_spec)
})


Chaining
Every proxy verb returns the proxy invisibly, so calls chain:
observeEvent(input$reset_view, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    clear_filters() |>
    sort_rows("study", "none") |>
    toggle_group("all", collapsed = FALSE)
})

title: Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
text:
Complete Example App
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)
library(dplyr)

meta_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("ADVANCE", "SPRINT", "ACCORD", "ONTARGET", "HOPE-3",
            "EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR"),
  drug_class = c("ACEi", "Intensive BP", "Intensive BP", "ARB", "Statin",
                 "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i"),
  hr    = c(0.91, 0.75, 0.88, 0.94, 0.90, 0.62, 0.86, 0.93, 0.74, 0.75),
  lower = c(0.83, 0.64, 0.76, 0.86, 0.82, 0.49, 0.75, 0.84, 0.65, 0.65),
  upper = c(1.01, 0.87, 1.01, 1.02, 0.98, 0.77, 0.97, 1.03, 0.85, 0.86),
  n     = c(11140, 9361, 10251, 25620, 12705, 7020, 10142, 17160, 4744, 3730)
)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Interactive Forest Plot"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      width = 3,
      h4("Filters"),
      selectInput("drug_class", "Drug Class",
        choices = c("All", unique(meta_data$drug_class)), selected = "All"),
      sliderInput("min_n", "Minimum Sample Size",
        min = 0, max = 20000, value = 0, step = 1000),
      hr(),
      h4("Display"),
      selectInput("theme", "Theme",
        choices = c("Default" = "default", "JAMA" = "jama", "Lancet" = "lancet")),
      actionButton("sort_hr", "Sort by HR"),
      actionButton("reset",   "Reset View")
    ),
    mainPanel(width = 9, tabvizOutput("plot", height = "600px"))
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  plot_data <- reactive({
    d <- meta_data
    if (input$drug_class != "All") d <- dplyr::filter(d, drug_class == input$drug_class)
    dplyr::filter(d, n >= input$min_n)
  })

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(plot_data(),
      label = "study",
      group = "drug_class",
      columns = list(
        col_numeric("n", "N"),
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")
      )
    )
  })

  observe({
    set_theme(tabviz_proxy("plot"), switch(input$theme,
      "jama" = "jama", "lancet" = "lancet", "default"))
  })

  sort_state <- reactiveVal("none")
  observeEvent(input$sort_hr, {
    new_dir <- switch(sort_state(), "none" = "asc", "asc" = "desc", "desc" = "none")
    sort_state(new_dir)
    sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", new_dir)
  })

  observeEvent(input$reset, {
    updateSelectInput(session, "drug_class", selected = "All")
    updateSliderInput(session, "min_n", value = 0)
    sort_state("none")
    tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
      clear_filters() |>
      sort_rows("hr", "none")
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Proxy vs Re-render








Use case
Approach
Reason




Sort / filter existing rows
Proxy
Smooth, no flicker


Change theme / zoom
Proxy
set_theme() / set_zoom() route through the store


Add, remove, resize columns
Proxy
Column verbs are wired through the store


Completely different dataset (new columns/types)
Re-render or update_data() with a fresh WebSpec



Toggle groups
Proxy
UI-state only

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Best Practices

Create proxies inside reactive contexts, not at app top level.
Debounce slider-driven inputs with shiny::debounce().
Prefer proxy verbs for interactive updates — they preserve scroll and selection.
Reach for full re-render only when the column structure or data schema changes.
Chain proxy calls for atomic UI updates.

title: Row Groups
text: Organize your forest plot into collapsible sections using the group parameter. Ideal for meta-analyses with natural categories like study design, region, or subpopulation.

title: Row Groups
section: What you can pass to group
text:
What you can pass to group







Form
Effect




group = "region"
Single flat grouping — one collapsible header per unique value


group = c("region", "country")
Hierarchical nesting — outer level collapses inner levels


group = list(web_group("North", collapsed = FALSE), web_group("South", collapsed = TRUE))
Explicit definitions; lets you set initial collapse state and custom labels

title: Row Groups
text:
Basic Grouping
Pass a column name to group to create collapsible sections:


Code
grouped_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A1", "Study A2", "Study B1", "Study B2", "Study B3"),
  region = c("North", "North", "South", "South", "South"),
  or = c(0.8, 0.9, 1.1, 1.3, 0.95),
  lower = c(0.6, 0.7, 0.85, 1.0, 0.7),
  upper = c(1.1, 1.15, 1.4, 1.7, 1.3)
)

forest_plot(grouped_data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  group = "region",
  null_value = 1
)






Click the group headers to collapse or expand sections. Groups are rendered with:

Distinct header rows with chevron indicators
Automatic indentation of child rows
Subtle background shading for visual hierarchy

title: Row Groups
text:
Hierarchical Grouping
For multi-level organization, pass multiple column names as a vector. tabviz creates nested, collapsible hierarchies:


Code
hierarchical_data <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    "Northeast Trial", "Southeast Trial", "Midwest Trial",
    "Ontario Study", "Quebec Study",
    "England RCT", "Scotland RCT",
    "Bavaria Trial", "Berlin Trial"
  ),
  region = c(
    rep("Americas", 5),
    rep("Europe", 4)
  ),
  country = c(
    rep("USA", 3), rep("Canada", 2),
    rep("UK", 2), rep("Germany", 2)
  ),
  or = c(0.82, 0.91, 0.78, 0.85, 0.88, 0.92, 0.87, 0.79, 0.84),
  lower = c(0.68, 0.75, 0.62, 0.70, 0.72, 0.76, 0.71, 0.64, 0.68),
  upper = c(0.99, 1.10, 0.98, 1.03, 1.07, 1.11, 1.07, 0.98, 1.04)
)

forest_plot(hierarchical_data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  group = c("region", "country"),
  null_value = 1, scale = "log",
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)






Each level of the hierarchy:

Can be collapsed independently
Has progressively deeper indentation
Uses subtle visual differentiation (font weight, background)

title: Row Groups
Row Groups
text:
Starting Collapsed
To have groups start in a collapsed state, you’ll need to use explicit group definitions with web_group():


Code
forest_plot(grouped_data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  group = list(
    web_group("North", collapsed = FALSE),
    web_group("South", collapsed = TRUE)
  ),
  null_value = 1
)


This is useful for large datasets where you want users to expand only the sections they’re interested in.

title: Row Groups
text:
Grouping vs Row Styling
tabviz provides two complementary ways to organize data:








Feature
Purpose
Best For




group
Automatic collapsible sections from column values
Categorical data with natural groupings


row_type
Manual section headers, summaries, spacers
Structured layouts with subtotals



Use group when your data has a grouping column. Use row_type when you need precise control over the layout structure, such as meta-analysis summary rows. See Row Styling for the manual approach.
You can combine both approaches—use group for automatic organization and row_type for summary diamonds within each group.

title: Row Groups
text:
See Also

Row Styling — Manual row types, headers, summaries, and visual styling
Themes — Customize group header appearance
forest_plot() — Complete argument reference

title: Export System Architecture
text: A single TypeScript SVG generator drives every static export, whether triggered from R via save_plot() or from the in-browser download button. This page explains why and how that pipeline works, what the V8 bundle does, and where to extend it.

title: Export System Architecture
text:
Overview
tabviz uses a unified rendering architecture for static image export. The same JavaScript SVG generator powers both:

R-side: save_plot() function (via the V8 package)
Web-side: Download button in interactive plots

This ensures consistent output regardless of where the export is triggered.
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   svg-generator.ts                       │
│         (Pure-data SVG generator, no DOM needed)         │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
       ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
       │                               │
       ▼                               ▼
┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
│  Web (browser)   │         │   R (via V8)     │
│  DownloadButton  │         │   save_plot()    │
└──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘

title: Export System Architecture
text:
Why This Design?

Single Source of Truth
Instead of maintaining two separate renderers (one in R, one in JavaScript), we have a single TypeScript implementation that:

Reduces code duplication
Guarantees visual consistency between exports
Makes bug fixes and improvements apply to both platforms



No Browser Dependency in R
The SVG generator is DOM-free — it takes WebSpec JSON data and outputs an SVG string through pure computation. This means:

Works in R via the lightweight V8 JavaScript engine
No need for headless Chrome/Chromium
Fast execution without browser overhead
Works offline without any system dependencies beyond V8

title: Export System Architecture
text:
Components

svg-generator.ts
Located at srcjs/src/lib/svg-generator.ts, this is the core rendering engine.
Key functions:
// Main entry point
generateSVG(spec: WebSpec, options?: ExportOptions): string

// Internal helpers
computeLayout(spec: WebSpec): Layout
renderUnifiedTableRow(row, columns, layout, theme): string
renderInterval(row, yPosition, xScale, theme): string
renderDiamond(point, lower, upper, yPosition, ...): string
renderForestAxis(xScale, layout, theme, axisLabel): string
renderVizBar(...) / renderVizBoxplot(...) / renderVizViolin(...): string
Design constraints:

No DOM access — Cannot use document, window, canvas.measureText(), etc.
No external dependencies — Pure TypeScript, bundled as standalone IIFE
Deterministic output — Same input always produces same SVG



Text Measurement and Auto-Width
Since V8 has no DOM, we can’t use canvas.measureText() for precise text width calculation. Instead, width-utils.ts provides character-aware estimation:
function estimateTextWidth(text: string, fontSize: number): number {
  let width = 0;
  for (const char of text) {
    if ("il1.,;:|!()[]{}' ".includes(char)) {
      width += fontSize * 0.35;  // Narrow characters
    } else if ("mwMW@%".includes(char)) {
      width += fontSize * 0.85;  // Wide characters
    } else if (char >= "0" && char <= "9") {
      width += fontSize * 0.6;   // Tabular numbers
    } else {
      width += fontSize * 0.55;  // Average characters
    }
  }
  return width;
}
Auto-width calculation: Both web and SVG renderers now calculate column widths from actual content. For each column with width="auto":

Measure the header text
Measure all cell values using the proper formatted text (e.g., “0.76 (0.68, 0.85)” for intervals)
Apply padding (28px) and constraints (min 60px, max 600px)

This ensures columns are wide enough to display their content without truncation, matching the behavior of the interactive web renderer.


Build Outputs
The SVG generator is bundled twice:








File
Purpose
Size




inst/htmlwidgets/tabviz.js
Full widget bundle (includes Svelte UI)
~142 KB


inst/js/svg-generator.js
Standalone for V8 (export only)
~11 KB



Build commands:
cd srcjs
bun run build          # Both bundles
bun run build:widget   # Widget only
bun run build:v8       # V8 bundle only

title: Export System Architecture
text:
R Integration

save_plot()
save_plot(spec, "output.svg", width = 800, height = NULL)
Process:

Serialize WebSpec to JSON via serialize_spec()
Create V8 context and load svg-generator.js
Call generateSVG(specJson, options)
Write SVG string to file (or convert to PDF/PNG via rsvg)

Dependencies:

V8 — JavaScript engine (Suggests)
rsvg — SVG to PDF/PNG conversion (Suggests, optional)



Code Path
save_plot(spec, file)
    │
    ├── serialize_spec(spec)     # R: WebSpec → JSON
    │
    ├── generate_svg_v8(json)    # R: Load V8, call JS
    │       │
    │       └── generateSVG()    # JS: JSON → SVG string
    │
    └── writeLines() / rsvg      # R: Save to file

title: Export System Architecture
Export System Architecture
Export System Architecture
Export System Architecture
text:
Troubleshooting

“SVG generator JavaScript file not found”
The V8 bundle hasn’t been built. Run:
cd srcjs && bun run build


Text alignment looks off
The text width estimation is character-aware but may still be slightly off for unusual fonts. Solutions:

Explicitly set column widths with col_text("field", width = 150)
Use a standard font family in the theme (Inter, Arial, system-ui)
Increase or decrease padding via the AUTO_WIDTH.PADDING constant in rendering-constants.ts



Colors don’t match the interactive plot
Ensure you’re using the same theme. The generator resolves all CSS variables to literal color values at export time.

title: Themes
text:
Built-in theme presets, the web_theme() custom-theme constructor, the four core modifiers (set_inputs(), set_variants(), set_spacing(), set_theme_field()), and package_themes() for listing every shipped theme. Pass any theme to tabviz(theme = ...) or apply it after the fact with set_theme().
For the conceptual model see the Themes guide and the Styling Cascade concepts page.

title: Themes
text:
Preset themes
web_theme_*() builders return a fully resolved WebTheme. They take no arguments — chain modifiers to customize.



Theme
Purpose
Notable defaults




web_theme_cochrane()
The package default
Cochrane teal + warm-coral accent, Inter sans-serif, comfortable density


web_theme_lancet()
Lancet-style editorial
Navy + refined old-gold + warm cream, Georgia serif


web_theme_jama()
JAMA / print journals
Pure black-and-white, ultra-compact, strong dividers, Arial


web_theme_dark()
Dark-mode viewing
Catppuccin Mocha-inspired canvas, pastel markers



tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")),
       theme = web_theme_lancet())

web_theme_cochrane()


web_theme_lancet()


web_theme_jama()


web_theme_dark()
All preset builders take no arguments and return a fully resolved WebTheme.

title: Themes
text:
package_themes()
Returns a named list of every theme distributed with the package — handy for the enable_themes argument of web_interaction() or for iterating in tests.
package_themes()
# $cochrane, $lancet, $jama, $dark

title: Themes
text:
web_theme()
Custom-theme constructor. Extends a base_theme with named lists of Tier 1 inputs and per-table variants. Resolves the theme before returning.
web_theme(
  name = "custom",
  inputs = NULL,        # Named list of ThemeInputs property overrides
  variants = NULL,      # Named list of ThemeVariants property overrides
  base_theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




name
string
"custom"
Theme name (shown in the in-widget switcher and in source export).


inputs
named list
NULL
Tier 1 input overrides. Any ThemeInputs field is valid: neutral, brand, brand_deep, accent, accent_deep, status_*, series_anchors, font_body, font_display, font_mono. Unset fields inherit from base_theme.


variants
named list
NULL
Per-table variant overrides: density (compact / comfortable / spacious), header_style (light / bold), first_column_style (default / bold).


base_theme
WebTheme
web_theme_cochrane()
Starting point. The inputs and variants lists are applied on top.



Example — Lancet-inspired theme with bold headers:
custom <- web_theme(
  name = "navy-bold",
  inputs = list(
    brand          = "#1F3A5F",
    brand_deep     = "#0F2138",
    accent         = "#B08938",
    series_anchors = c("#1F3A5F", "#B08938", "#6B8E7F"),
    font_body      = "Charter, Georgia, serif"
  ),
  variants = list(density = "comfortable", header_style = "bold")
)

title: Themes
text:
Theme modifiers
All modifiers operate on a WebTheme and return a new (re-resolved) WebTheme. Chainable via the pipe.

set_inputs()
Update Tier 1 customer-facing inputs. Cascade-aware: changing brand clears brand_deep so it re-mirrors; changing series_anchors rebuilds the slot bundles. The pooled-effect diamond reads from series[1], so anchoring it is just a matter of setting the first series_anchors entry.
set_inputs(
  theme,
  neutral, brand, brand_deep, accent, accent_deep,
  status_positive, status_negative, status_warning, status_info,
  series_anchors,
  font_body, font_display, font_mono
)








Argument
Type
Description




theme
WebTheme
Theme to modify


neutral
chr[5]
5-step ramp, lightest → darkest


brand
hex
Brand fill color (markers, accents)


brand_deep
hex
Dark companion for text/strokes; defaults to brand


accent
hex
Chrome accent (hover, selection, links)


accent_deep
hex
Dark companion; defaults to accent


status_positive, status_negative, status_warning, status_info
hex
Semantic status colors


series_anchors
chr[]
Per-effect anchor colors (variable length). The first anchor also drives the pooled-effect diamond.


font_body
string
Primary CSS font-family


font_display
string
Optional title face; defaults to font_body


font_mono
string
Optional monospace face





set_variants()
Flip per-table toggles. Density change resets spacing tokens so the new preset’s values flow through.
set_variants(theme, density, header_style, first_column_style)








Argument
Values
Description




density
"compact" / "comfortable" / "spacious"
Drives every spacing token


header_style
"light" / "bold"
Picks between light header on muted surface and bold header on brand_deep


first_column_style
"default" / "bold"
Excel-style emphasized first column when "bold"





set_spacing()
Per-token spacing override on top of the active density preset. Set what you care about; the rest cascade from density.
set_spacing(
  theme,
  row_height, header_height, padding, container_padding, axis_gap,
  column_group_padding, row_group_padding, cell_padding_x,
  footer_gap, title_subtitle_gap, bottom_margin, indent_per_level
)
All values are numeric (pixels).


set_theme_field()
Generic deep setter. Walks the theme tree by path and replaces the leaf with value, then re-resolves. Use this for any field not covered by the typed setters above.
set_theme_field(theme, path, value)







Argument
Description




theme
A WebTheme


path
Character or mixed character/integer vector. Strings are property names; integers index list-typed properties (currently only series).


value
New value for the leaf field



Examples:
# Pin row-group L1 background regardless of theme
theme |> set_theme_field(c("row_group", "L1", "bg"), "#EEEEEE")

# Override series 2's stroke without touching anything else
theme |> set_theme_field(c("series", 2L, "stroke"), "#444444")

# Set Tier 1 inputs surgically
theme |> set_theme_field(c("inputs", "brand"), "#FF0000")

# Tweak axis config
theme |> set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
theme |> set_theme_field(c("axis", "tick_count"), 5)

# Pin layout config
theme |> set_theme_field(c("layout", "container_border_radius"), 4)
Re-resolution preserves explicit overrides on subsequent edits. set_inputs(brand = "...") after pinning surface.base will refresh series colors but leave surface.base pinned.

title: Themes
text:
Switching themes on a spec



Verb
Operates on
Purpose




set_theme()
WebSpec, htmlwidget, proxy
Swap to a preset name or WebTheme object


selectable_themes()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Curate the in-widget theme switcher



# Apply by name
plot |> set_theme("jama")

# Apply by object
custom <- web_theme(inputs = list(brand = "#1F3A5F"))
plot |> set_theme(custom)

title: Themes
text:
Worked examples

Lancet-style with custom Tier 1 input
my_lancet <- web_theme(
  name = "my-lancet",
  inputs = list(
    brand          = "#0066CC",
    accent         = "#CC9900",
    series_anchors = c("#0066CC", "#CC9900", "#669933"),
    font_body      = "Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif",
    font_display   = "Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif"
  ),
  variants = list(density = "comfortable")
)

# Swap presets, then tweak
custom <- web_theme_lancet() |>
  set_inputs(brand = "#0066CC") |>
  set_variants(density = "compact") |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)


High-contrast print theme
print_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "print",
  inputs = list(
    neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FFFFFF", "#FAFAFA", "#666666", "#1A1A1A"),
    brand          = "#000000",
    accent         = "#000000",
    series_anchors = c("#1A1A1A", "#4D4D4D", "#808080", "#B3B3B3"),
    font_body      = "'Times New Roman', serif",
    font_display   = "'Times New Roman', serif"
  ),
  variants = list(density = "compact"),
  base_theme = web_theme_jama()
) |>
  set_theme_field(c("row_group", "L1", "text", "weight"), 700) |>
  set_theme_field(c("row_group", "L1", "border_bottom"), TRUE)


Dark dashboard theme
dark_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "dashboard-dark",
  base_theme = web_theme_dark(),
  inputs = list(
    brand          = "#10B981",
    accent         = "#10B981",
    series_anchors = c("#10B981", "#3B82F6", "#F59E0B", "#EF4444")
  ),
  variants = list(density = "spacious", header_style = "bold")
)

title: Themes
text:
Theme structure (S7)
A WebTheme is an S7 object with the following top-level properties. For the conceptual model and how the cascades compose, see Styling Cascade and S7 Architecture.









Property
Type
Tier
Description




name
string
—
Theme identifier


inputs
ThemeInputs
1
Customer-facing inputs


variants
ThemeVariants
1
Per-table toggles


surface
Surfaces
2
base / muted / raised


content
Content
2
primary / secondary / muted / inverse


divider
Dividers
2
subtle / strong


accent
AccentRoles
2
default / muted / tint_subtle / tint_medium


status
StatusColors
2
positive / negative / warning / info


series
list<SlotBundle>
2
Per-anchor 7-field bundle


summary
SlotBundle
2
Pooled-effect diamond bundle


text
TextRoles
2
8 named role bundles


spacing
SpacingTokens
2
12 density-derived numerics


annotation
AnnotationCluster
3
title / subtitle / caption / footnote


header
HeaderCluster
3
light + bold variants


column_group
ColumnGroupCluster
3
Column-group header


row_group
RowGroupCluster
3
L1 / L2 / L3 tiers


row
RowCluster
3
base / alt / hover / selected + emphasis / muted / accent


cell
CellCluster
3
bg / fg / border / text


first_column
FirstColumnCluster
3
plain + bold variants


plot
PlotScaffold
3
axis / tick / gridline / reference + mark sizes


marks
MarksRecipes
3
Per-mark slot-bundle recipes


axis
AxisConfig
config
Forest x-axis range/ticks/grid


layout
Layout
config
Plot width, container border

title: tabviz()
text: tabviz() is the main entry point for building interactive table visualizations. It returns an htmlwidget that renders in R Markdown, Quarto, Shiny, or the RStudio Viewer. Use tabviz() whenever you want full control over which columns appear (text, numeric, badges, forest plots, sparklines, etc.). For a quick forest-only plot driven by point / lower / upper arguments, see forest_plot().

title: tabviz()
text:
Signature
tabviz(
  data,
  label = NULL,
  label_header = "Study",
  group = NULL,
  columns = NULL,
  extra_columns = NULL,
  available_exclude = NULL,
  title = NULL, subtitle = NULL, caption = NULL, footnote = NULL,
  # Row styling (column name or formula)
  row_bold = NULL, row_italic = NULL,
  row_color = NULL, row_bg = NULL,
  row_badge = NULL, row_icon = NULL,
  row_indent = NULL, row_type = NULL,
  row_emphasis = NULL, row_muted = NULL, row_accent = NULL,
  # Marker styling (column name or formula)
  marker_color = NULL, marker_shape = NULL,
  marker_opacity = NULL, marker_size = NULL,
  # Theme & interaction
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  interaction = NULL,
  plot_position = NULL,
  row_height = NULL,
  banding = NULL,
  width = NULL, height = NULL, elementId = NULL,
  # Splitting
  split_by = NULL, shared_axis = FALSE, shared_column_widths = FALSE,
  .spec_only = FALSE
)

title: tabviz()
text:
Arguments

Data



Argument
Type
Default
Description




data
data.frame
required
A data.frame, data.table, or tibble.


label
character(1)
NULL
Column name for row labels. When NULL, row numbers are used.


label_header
character(1)
"Study"
Header text for the label column. The literal default "Study" is auto-prettified from the label field name when applicable.


group
character / list
NULL
Grouping spec. A single column name (flat), a vector (hierarchical), or a list of web_group() objects. See Grouping.


columns
list
NULL
List of column specs from col_*() / viz_*() helpers.


extra_columns
list
NULL
Pre-configured columns hidden by default but available in the interactive column picker.


available_exclude
character
NULL
Data columns to omit from the column picker (e.g. internal/sensitive fields).





Titles









Argument
Type
Default
Description




title
character(1)
NULL
Main title (above the plot).


subtitle
character(1)
NULL
Subtitle (below title).


caption
character(1)
NULL
Caption (below plot).


footnote
character(1)
NULL
Footnote (below caption, italicized).





Row styling
All row-styling arguments accept either a column name (string) that holds the per-row value, or a formula evaluated against data (e.g. ~ pvalue < 0.05).









Argument
Value type
Default
Description




row_bold
logical
NULL
Bold whole row.


row_italic
logical
NULL
Italicize row.


row_color
CSS color
NULL
Row text color.


row_bg
CSS color
NULL
Row background color.


row_badge
character
NULL
Badge text on the label cell.


row_icon
emoji/unicode
NULL
Icon on the label cell.


row_indent
numeric
NULL
Indentation level.


row_type
character
NULL
One of "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".


row_emphasis
logical
NULL
Bold + foreground color.


row_muted
logical
NULL
Muted (lighter, reduced prominence).


row_accent
logical
NULL
Theme accent color.





Marker styling
Same column-name-or-formula pattern as row styling:









Argument
Value type
Default
Description




marker_color
CSS color
NULL
Marker fill color.


marker_shape
character
NULL
One of "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle".


marker_opacity
numeric [0,1]
NULL
Marker opacity.


marker_size
numeric
NULL
Driver for marker size — pass raw weights (e.g. study weights 0–100). The renderer normalizes via 0.5 + sqrt(w/100) * 1.5.





Theme, interaction, layout









Argument
Type
Default
Description




theme
WebTheme
web_theme_cochrane()
Theme object (use a web_theme_*() builder).


interaction
InteractionSpec
NULL
Use web_interaction(). When NULL, default_interaction_for_theme(theme) is used (full interactivity).


plot_position
character(1)
NULL
 No effect. Column order in columns = list(...) controls placement.


row_height
numeric(1)
NULL
Row height in pixels.


banding
character(1)
NULL
Row banding mode: "none", "row", "group" (deepest level, default), or "group-n" (e.g. "group-1" for outermost). Overrides the banding on theme. Use the viewer’s settings panel (gear icon) to adjust interactively.


width
character/numeric
NULL
Widget width (auto).


height
character/numeric
NULL
Widget height (auto).


elementId
character(1)
NULL
HTML element id.





Splitting









Argument
Type
Default
Description




split_by
character
NULL
Column name(s) to split into one plot per unique value/combination, returning a SplitForest widget.


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
When split_by is set, share an axis range across all sub-plots.


shared_column_widths
logical(1)
FALSE
When split_by is set, stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up when screenshots are stacked. Added in v0.16.





Other









Argument
Type
Default
Description




.spec_only
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, returns the WebSpec object instead of an htmlwidget — useful for programmatic manipulation before rendering.

title: tabviz()
text:
Deprecated arguments






ImportantRemoved from the tabviz() surface in 0.9.0



The arguments below are still accepted for one cycle but emit lifecycle::deprecate_warn(). They have moved to dedicated, more discoverable homes.





Old argument
Replacement




tabviz(axis_range = ...)
Pass axis_range to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(axis_ticks = ...)
Pass axis_ticks to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(axis_gridlines = ...)
Pass axis_gridlines to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(zoom = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(zoom = ...).


tabviz(auto_fit = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(auto_fit = ...).


tabviz(max_width = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(max_width = ...).


tabviz(max_height = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(max_height = ...).


tabviz(show_zoom_controls = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(show_controls = ...).


tabviz(weight = ...)
Use marker_size = ....

title: tabviz()
text:
Return value
An htmlwidget. When .spec_only = TRUE, returns a WebSpec instead. When split_by is set, returns a split-table widget backed by a SplitForest.

title: tabviz()
text:
Grouping
Three modes of group:
# Flat grouping
tabviz(data, group = "category", ...)

# Hierarchical grouping
tabviz(data, group = c("region", "country"), ...)

# Explicit group definitions
tabviz(data,
  group = list(
    web_group("cardio", label = "Cardiovascular"),
    web_group("renal",  label = "Renal Outcomes", collapsed = TRUE)
  ),
  ...)

title: tabviz()
text:
Examples


Code
meta <- data.frame(
  study = c("Smith 2020", "Jones 2021", "Lee 2022"),
  drug  = c("A", "A", "B"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
  lo    = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
  hi    = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10),
  n     = c(245, 180, 320)
)

# Minimal: forest column + a couple of data columns
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)








Code
# Row styling: bold significant rows, color the favourable ones green
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  row_bold  = ~ hi < 1,
  row_color = ~ ifelse(hi < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)








Code
# Spec-only: build, mutate fluently, then render
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
  .spec_only = TRUE
) |>
  set_theme("jama") |>
  set_marker_style(color = "drug") |>
  forest_plot()

title: tabviz()
text:
Related

forest_plot() — convenience wrapper around tabviz() + viz_forest()
Columns — every col_*() and viz_*() helper
Styling modifiers — set_row_style(), set_marker_style(), set_zoom()
Themes — web_theme_*() builders and set_*() modifiers
Interaction — web_interaction()
Export — save_plot() for SVG / PNG / PDF

title: Columns
text:
Reference for every column-defining helper: data columns (col_*()), per-row visualization columns (viz_*()), the low-level web_col() constructor, column groups, and the per-effect helpers used by multi-effect viz columns.
All col_*() helpers also accept the cell-styling arguments documented in Cell-level styling below: bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent, plus align, header_align, wrap, sortable, na_text, tooltip, and formatter.

title: Columns
text:
Per-row visualization columns (viz_*)
These columns plot one image per row with a shared axis across rows. They define their own effects and are not sortable.

viz_forest()
Forest plot column (point + CI). Either pass point / lower / upper for a single effect, or effects = list(effect_forest(...), ...) for several effects overlaid in the same column.
viz_forest(
  header = NULL,
  show_header = TRUE,
  header_align = "center",
  width = NULL,
  point = NULL, lower = NULL, upper = NULL,
  effects = NULL,
  scale = c("linear", "log"),
  null_value = NULL,
  axis_label = "Effect",
  axis_range = NULL,
  axis_ticks = NULL,
  axis_gridlines = FALSE,
  show_axis = TRUE,
  annotations = NULL,
  shared_axis = NULL,
  ...
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




header
character(1)
NULL
Falls back to single-effect label, then blank for multi-effect.

logical(1)
TRUE

"center"

NULL
Single-effect column refs (mutually exclusive with effects).

list of effect_forest()

Multi-effect spec.

"linear"
"linear" or "log".

Reference line.

In split forests, opt this column in/out of the split-level shared axis.

effect_forest(point, lower, upper,
              label = NULL, color = NULL,

NoteNo per-effect size



In multi-effect plots, marker size is row-driven (via set_marker_style(size = ...) or marker_size on tabviz()) so weights remain comparable across effects.




viz_bar()
viz_bar(...,                  # one or more effect_bar() objects
        header = NULL, show_header = TRUE, header_align = "center",
        width = NULL,
        scale = c("linear", "log"),
        axis_range = NULL, axis_ticks = NULL,
        axis_gridlines = FALSE,
        axis_label = "Value", show_axis = TRUE)

effect_bar()
effect_bar(value, label = NULL, color = NULL, opacity = NULL)



viz_boxplot()
viz_boxplot(...,              # one or more effect_boxplot() objects
            header = NULL, show_header = TRUE, header_align = "center",
            width = NULL,
            scale = c("linear", "log"),
            axis_range = NULL, axis_ticks = NULL,
            axis_gridlines = FALSE,
            show_outliers = TRUE,
            whisker_type = c("iqr", "minmax"),
            axis_label = "Value", show_axis = TRUE)

effect_boxplot()
Two modes — provide either data (raw array column) or all five summary stats.
# From raw array data (auto-compute quartiles)
effect_boxplot(data,
               outliers = NULL, label = NULL,
               color = NULL, opacity = 0.7)

# From pre-computed summary stats
effect_boxplot(min, q1, median, q3, max,
               outliers = NULL, label = NULL,
               color = NULL, opacity = 0.7)






WarningRenamed in 0.9.0



fill_opacity is now opacity (matching effect_forest() / effect_bar()). The old name still works with a deprecation warning.





viz_violin()
viz_violin(...,                # one or more effect_violin() objects
           header = NULL, show_header = TRUE, header_align = "center",
           width = NULL,

           axis_range = NULL, axis_ticks = NULL,
           axis_gridlines = FALSE,
           bandwidth = NULL,
           show_median = TRUE, show_quartiles = FALSE,
           axis_label = "Value", show_axis = TRUE)

effect_violin()
effect_violin(data, label = NULL, color = NULL, opacity = 0.5)

fill_opacity is now opacity. The old name still works with a deprecation warning.

title: Columns
text:
Column groups

col_group()
Wrap several columns under a shared header.
col_group(header, ...)



Argument
Type
Description




header
character(1)
Group header label.


...
ColumnSpec
One or more col_*() / viz_*() columns.



col_group("Treatment Effect",
  col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
  col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "95% CI")
)

title: Columns
text:
Low-level constructor

web_col()
The constructor every col_*() helper delegates to. Use this directly only when you need a column type the helpers don’t cover.
web_col(field,
        header = NULL,
        type = c("text", "numeric", "interval", "bar", "pvalue",
                 "sparkline", "icon", "badge", "stars", "img",
                 "reference", "range", "forest", "heatmap",
                 "progress", "viz_bar", "viz_boxplot",
                 "viz_violin", "custom"),
        width = NULL,
        align = NULL, header_align = NULL,
        show_header = NULL,
        wrap = FALSE, sortable = TRUE,
        options = list(),
        na_text = NULL,
        # Cell styling
        bold = NULL, italic = NULL,
        color = NULL, bg = NULL,
        badge = NULL, icon = NULL,
        emphasis = NULL, muted = NULL, accent = NULL)
Returns a ColumnSpec.

title: Columns
text:
Cell-level styling
Every col_*() helper accepts the same set of cell-styling arguments via .... Each takes either a column name (resolved at render time) or a formula evaluated against the row (use .x to refer to the column’s own value).



Argument
Resolved value
Effect




bold
logical
Bold text.


italic
logical
Italic text.


color
CSS color
Text color.


bg
CSS color
Cell background.


badge
character
Inline badge in the cell.


icon
emoji/unicode
Icon next to value.


emphasis
logical
Theme-aware emphasis (bold + foreground).


muted
logical
Theme-aware muted (lighter).


accent
logical
Theme accent color.



# Bold significant p-values, color them
col_pvalue("pval",
           bold  = ~ .x < 0.05,
           color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#71717a"))

# Reference another column
col_numeric("hr", "HR",
            color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#71717a"))

title: Columns
text:
Alignment
Two slots: align for body cells, header_align for the header. Defaults are type-aware:







Helper
Body align default




col_text, col_numeric, col_pvalue, col_bar, col_interval, col_sparkline, col_percent, col_reference, col_heatmap, col_progress, col_date
"left"


col_icon, col_badge, col_stars, col_img
"center"


col_range, col_currency
"right"


viz_forest
header "center"


viz_bar
header "left"


viz_boxplot, viz_violin
header "center"



header_align defaults to “follow the body” — set it explicitly to decouple.

title: Columns
text:
Examples


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study  = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C"),
  hr     = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.68),
  lower  = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.52),
  upper  = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.89),
  n      = c(245, 180, 320),
  weight = c(35, 28, 37),
  pval   = c(0.018, 0.092, 0.003)
)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_bar("weight"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE)
  )
)






# Column groups
columns = list(
  col_n("n"),
  col_group("Treatment Effect",
    col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "95% CI")
  ),
  col_pvalue("pval")
)

title: Columns
text:
Related

tabviz() — passes a columns list of these helpers
Styling modifiers — set_column_style() for after-the-fact per-cell styling
Annotations — refline() for viz_forest(annotations = ...)

title: split_table()
text:
split_table() slices a spec or widget into a SplitForest — one sub-plot per unique value (or combination of values) of the splitting column(s). The resulting object renders with sidebar navigation in the browser, can be exported one file per sub-plot via save_split_table(), and supports a shared-axis option for cross-subgroup comparison.
The same effect is available inline on tabviz() and forest_plot() via the split_by / shared_axis arguments — split_table() is the explicit constructor used when you want a SplitForest object to inspect, modify, or hand to save_split_table().

title: split_table()
text:
Signature
split_table(x, by, shared_axis = FALSE, shared_column_widths = FALSE, ...)

title: split_table()
text:
Arguments









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / data.frame
required
Source. A data frame is fed through web_spec() first.


by
character
required
Column name(s) to split on. A single string creates one plot per unique value; a vector creates a hierarchical navigation tree (outermost first).


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, all sub-plots use the same x-axis range so subgroup effects can be compared at a glance.


shared_column_widths
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up tile-to-tile when screenshots are stacked in slides / PowerPoint. Widths are computed once from the combined data. Added in v0.16.


...
—
—
Forwarded to web_spec() when x is a data frame.

title: split_table()
text:
Return value
A SplitForest containing one WebSpec per split combination, the navigation tree, the split variables, and (when shared_axis = TRUE) the shared axis range.

title: split_table()
text:
tabviz() / forest_plot() shortcut
Both top-level entry points accept split_by and shared_axis directly. They build a WebSpec first, call split_table() internally, then render the result.
# Equivalent
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest("or", "lo", "hi")),
       split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest("or", "lo", "hi")),
       .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  split_table(by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE) |>
  forest_plot()

title: split_table()
text:
Examples


Code
set.seed(42)
data <- data.frame(
  study  = paste0("Study ", 1:30),
  region = sample(c("Americas", "Europe", "Asia"), 30, replace = TRUE),
  or     = exp(rnorm(30, log(0.8), 0.3))
)
data$lower <- data$or * exp(-1.96 * 0.25)
data$upper <- data$or * exp( 1.96 * 0.25)

forest_plot(data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  split_by = "region",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1,
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)








Code
# Hierarchical split with nested sidebar
forest_plot(data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  split_by = c("sex", "age_group"),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)




Code
# Build the SplitForest explicitly to export
sf <- tabviz(data, label = "study",
             columns = list(viz_forest("or", "lower", "upper")),
             .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"), shared_axis = TRUE)

sf                              # printed summary of the SplitForest
save_split_table(sf, "out/")    # one file per sub-plot

title: split_table()
text:
Related

tabviz() and forest_plot() — both accept split_by directly
save_split_table() — export every sub-plot to a directory tree
Shiny bindings — splitTabvizOutput(), renderSplitTabviz(), split_tabviz_proxy()

title: Interaction
text: web_interaction() builds an InteractionSpec controlling what users can do with a tabviz widget — sorting, filtering, reordering, editing, exporting, theme switching. Three presets cover common cases (_full, _minimal, _publication).

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction()
web_interaction(
  show_legend = TRUE,
  enable_sort = TRUE,
  enable_collapse = TRUE,
  enable_select = TRUE,
  enable_hover = TRUE,
  enable_resize = TRUE,
  enable_export = TRUE,
  enable_filters = TRUE,
  enable_reorder_rows = TRUE,
  enable_reorder_columns = TRUE,
  enable_edit = TRUE,
  show_group_counts = FALSE,
  tooltip_fields = NULL,
  enable_themes = getOption("tabviz.enable_themes", "default")
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




show_legend
logical(1)
TRUE
Render the legend (multi-effect plots).


enable_sort
logical(1)
TRUE
Click headers to sort.


enable_collapse
logical(1)
TRUE
Click row-group headers to collapse / expand.


enable_select
logical(1)
TRUE
Click rows to select.


enable_hover
logical(1)
TRUE
Highlight rows on hover.


enable_resize
logical(1)
TRUE
Drag column borders to resize.


enable_export
logical(1)
TRUE
Show download / export button on hover.


enable_filters
logical(1)
TRUE
Per-column filter popovers in headers.


enable_reorder_rows
logical(1)
TRUE
Drag rows within a group, and row-groups among siblings.


enable_reorder_columns
logical(1)
TRUE
Drag columns within a group, and column-groups among top-level siblings.


enable_edit
logical(1)
TRUE
Double-click cells to edit; popover editor for forest-cell numerics. Also enables inline editing of title / subtitle / caption / footnote (v0.15+).


show_group_counts
logical(1)
FALSE
Append (n) after each row-group label.


tooltip_fields
character
NULL
Column names shown in the hover tooltip. NULL disables tooltips.


enable_themes
"default" / NULL / list of WebTheme
getOption("tabviz.enable_themes", "default")
Curate the theme switcher (see below).



Returns: an InteractionSpec.






Importantshow_filters was renamed in 0.9.0



The argument is now enable_filters — the column-filter UI is a user capability, not a static visibility toggle. The old show_filters is still accepted for one cycle and emits lifecycle::deprecate_warn().



enable_themes
Curates the in-widget theme switcher.







Value
Effect




"default"
Show every theme returned by package_themes().


NULL
Hide the switcher entirely.


list(WebTheme, ...)
Show exactly those themes. Named entries override the displayed name (e.g. list(Classical = web_theme_jama())). The spec’s active theme is auto-included so users can always revert.



# Two-theme curated menu, custom display names
web_interaction(enable_themes = list(
  Classical = web_theme_jama(),
  Modern    = web_theme_cochrane()
))
The fluent equivalent on a built spec is selectable_themes().

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction_full()
All capabilities on. This is what default_interaction_for_theme() returns, and what tabviz() falls back to when interaction = NULL.
web_interaction_full()

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction_minimal()
Read-only with hover highlighting only.
web_interaction_minimal()
# Equivalent to web_interaction() with every enable_* set to FALSE
# except enable_hover = TRUE; show_legend kept TRUE.

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction_publication()
Print-clean: nothing is interactive, no legend chrome.
web_interaction_publication()
# Every show_* and enable_* set to FALSE.
Use when:

Exporting screenshots for print publications.
Embedding in static documents where any UI noise is unwelcome.

title: Interaction
text:
default_interaction_for_theme()
default_interaction_for_theme(theme)
Returns web_interaction_full() regardless of theme — every shipped theme defaults to full interactivity. The function is exported for use in custom widget builders. Pass interaction = web_interaction_publication() (or _minimal()) explicitly for a quieter widget.

title: Interaction
text:
Examples


Code
data(glp1_trials)

# Hover-only preset
glp1_trials |>
  dplyr::filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
    interaction = web_interaction_minimal(),
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ))








Code
# Hover tooltips with selected fields
glp1_trials |>
  dplyr::filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
    interaction = web_interaction(tooltip_fields = c("drug", "n", "events", "pvalue")),
    footnote = "Hover over markers to see details",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ))






# Disable just one capability
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  interaction = web_interaction(enable_sort = FALSE),
  columns = list(...))

# Curated theme menu via the constructor
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  interaction = web_interaction(enable_themes = list(
    web_theme_jama(), web_theme_lancet()
  )),
  columns = list(...))

text:

set_theme() — change the active theme
Themes — package_themes() and the web_theme_*() builders
tabviz() — the interaction argument

title: Shiny Integration
text: Functions for embedding tabviz plots in Shiny applications and updating them in place from server code via proxies. The proxy pattern lets every fluent verb dispatch transparently — the same sort_rows() call works on a WebSpec, an htmlwidget, or a live tabviz_proxy.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Output and Render

tabvizOutput()
Create a Shiny output element for a tabviz plot.
tabvizOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "400px")









Argument
Type
Default
Description




outputId
character(1)
required
Output variable name.


width
character/numeric
"100%"
Widget width (CSS units).


height
character/numeric
"400px"
Widget height (CSS units).





renderTabviz()
renderTabviz(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




expr
expression
required
Returns a widget from tabviz().


env
environment
parent.frame()
Evaluation environment.


quoted
logical(1)
FALSE
Whether expr is already quoted.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Proxy Updates

tabviz_proxy()
Create a proxy to update a running tabviz widget without re-rendering it. Pass the proxy to any fluent verb (sort_rows(), filter_rows(), add_column(), set_theme(), …) to dispatch an update message to the browser.
tabviz_proxy(id, session = shiny::getDefaultReactiveDomain())









Argument
Type
Default
Description




id
character(1)
required
Widget (output) id.


session
Shiny session
getDefaultReactiveDomain()
Current reactive session.



Returns a tabviz_proxy object. Always create proxies inside a reactive context (observe, observeEvent, …), never at the top of server.


Verbs that accept a proxy
All fluent verbs dispatch on input type: static WebSpec / htmlwidget objects are mutated in R; tabviz_proxy objects send a custom message to the browser. See the full list in Styling and the Fluent API guide.







Category
Verbs




Columns
add_column(), remove_column(), move_column(), resize_column(), update_column()


Rows
sort_rows(), filter_rows(), clear_filters(), select_rows(), move_row()


Data
update_data() (proxy requires a WebSpec)


Groups
toggle_group()


Cells
set_cell(), set_row_label(), clear_edits() (proxy-only)


Global
set_theme(), set_zoom(), styling set_* verbs

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Split-Table Bindings

splitTabvizOutput()
splitTabvizOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "600px")
Same arguments as tabvizOutput() with a taller default height suited to the split-view’s sidebar layout.


renderSplitTabviz()
renderSplitTabviz(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)
expr should return a widget created from a split_table() result (or a forest_plot() / tabviz() call with split_by).


split_tabviz_proxy()
split_tabviz_proxy(id, session = shiny::getDefaultReactiveDomain())
Returns a split_tabviz_proxy object.


split_tabviz_select()
Select a sub-plot in a split-table tabviz.
split_tabviz_select(proxy, key)








Argument
Type
Description




proxy
split_tabviz_proxy
From split_tabviz_proxy().


key
character(1)
Sub-plot key (e.g. "Americas" or "Male__Young").

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Examples

Basic Shiny app


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Forest Plot Demo"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput("theme", "Theme:",
        choices = c("Default", "JAMA", "Lancet", "Modern")
      )
    ),
    mainPanel(
      tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  data <- data.frame(
    study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C"),
    hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
    lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
    upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10)
  )

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    theme <- switch(input$theme,
      "Default" = web_theme_cochrane(),
      "JAMA"    = web_theme_jama(),
      "Lancet"  = web_theme_lancet(),
      "Modern"  = web_theme_cochrane()
    )

    tabviz(data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      ),
      theme = theme
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)




Proxy updates (no re-render)


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tabvizOutput("plot"),
  actionButton("filter_btn", "Filter HR < 1"),
  actionButton("clear_btn",  "Clear Filter"),
  actionButton("sort_btn",   "Sort HR desc")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  data <- data.frame(
    study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
    hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 1.15, 0.91),
    lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.90, 0.75),
    upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.45, 1.10)
  )

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })

  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")

  observeEvent(input$filter_btn, filter_rows(proxy, "hr", "lt", 1))
  observeEvent(input$clear_btn,  clear_filters(proxy))
  observeEvent(input$sort_btn,   sort_rows(proxy, "hr", "desc"))
}




Replacing the dataset
update_data() on a proxy requires a WebSpec (not a raw data frame) so the new columns/types can be serialized. Build one with tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE).


Code
observeEvent(input$switch_dataset, {
  new_spec <- tabviz(
    get_dataset(input$dataset_name),
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ),
    .spec_only = TRUE
  )
  update_data(tabviz_proxy("plot"), new_spec)
})

text:

tabviz() — main entry point.

title: save_plot()
text:
Export a tabviz widget, WebSpec, or SplitForest to a static SVG, PDF, or PNG.
The pipeline runs entirely in R: the spec is serialized to JSON, the bundled JavaScript SVG generator (inst/js/svg-generator.js) is run via V8, and PDF/PNG outputs are produced from that SVG via rsvg.

title: save_plot()
text:
save_plot()
save_plot(x, file, width = 800, height = NULL, scale = 2, ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / SplitForest
required
The plot to export. SplitForest is dispatched to save_split_table().


file
character(1)
required
Output file path (or directory for SplitForest). Extension determines the format: .svg, .pdf, or .png.


width
numeric(1)
800
Plot width in pixels.


height
numeric(1)
NULL (auto)
Plot height in pixels; auto-calculated from content when NULL.


scale
numeric(1)
2
Scaling factor for PNG output (2 = retina).


...
—
—
Currently unused.



Returns: the output file path, invisibly.

Dependencies

SVG: V8 (always required for the generator).
PDF / PNG: also requires the rsvg package.

There is no headless-browser dependency.


Resolution guide for PNG



scale
Effective DPI
Use case




1
~72
Quick preview


2
~144
Standard web / screen (default)


3
~216
Print quality


4
~288
High-resolution print





Examples
p <- forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi", label = "study")

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg")                       # vector
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png", width = 1200, scale = 3)
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.pdf", width = 800)

title: save_plot()
text:
save_split_table()
Export every sub-plot in a SplitForest to a directory tree mirroring the split hierarchy.
save_split_table(x, path,
                 format = c("svg", "pdf", "png"),
                 width = 800, height = NULL, scale = 2,
                 ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
SplitForest / htmlwidget
required
A split-forest container or a widget produced with split_by.


path
character(1)
required
Output directory; created if it doesn’t exist.


format
choice
"svg"
"svg", "pdf", or "png".


width / height / scale
—
as in save_plot()
Forwarded to save_plot() for each sub-plot.


...
—
—
Currently unused.



Returns: a character vector of exported file paths, invisibly.

Output directory structure







Split
Files




split_by = "region"
path/North.svg, path/South.svg, …


split_by = c("region", "age")
path/North/North_Young.svg, path/North/North_Old.svg, …





Example
data |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
         columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper"))) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group")) |>
  save_split_table("output/plots", format = "svg")
save_plot() itself accepts a SplitForest and dispatches to save_split_table() automatically — pass either a directory path or a path with an extension to choose the format.

title: save_plot()
text:

web_interaction_publication() — quiet UI before exporting screenshots

title: Styling modifiers
text:
Fluent verbs that mutate a WebSpec, htmlwidget, or live Shiny tabviz_proxy to add row, column, marker, theme, and zoom styling. Every modifier returns the same kind of object it received, so they compose cleanly with |>.
For theme-level modifiers (palettes, typography, axis, layout) see Themes.

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_row_style()
Apply row-level styling driven by columns in the data.
set_row_style(
  x,
  bold = NULL,
  italic = NULL,
  color = NULL,
  bg = NULL,
  badge = NULL,
  icon = NULL,
  indent = NULL,
  type = NULL,
  weight = NULL,
  emphasis = NULL,
  muted = NULL,
  accent = NULL
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget
required
Spec or widget.


bold
column name
NULL
Logical column for bold rows.


italic
column name
NULL
Logical column for italic rows.


color
column name
NULL
CSS color column for row text.


bg
column name
NULL
CSS color column for row background.


badge
column name
NULL
Text column rendered as a badge on the label cell.


icon
column name
NULL
Emoji/unicode column rendered on the label cell.


indent
column name
NULL
Numeric column for label indentation.


type
column name
NULL
One of "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".


weight
column name
NULL
Numeric column driving marker size. Same shape as marker_size.


emphasis
column name
NULL
Logical column → bold + foreground color.


muted
column name
NULL
Logical column → muted styling.


accent
column name
NULL
Logical column → accent color.



Returns: modified input (WebSpec or htmlwidget).


Code
styled <- data.frame(
  label = c("Primary Endpoint", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Summary"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, 0.78),
  lo = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, 0.65),
  hi = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, 0.93),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "summary"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

tabviz(styled, label = "label",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1))
) |>
  set_row_style(type = "rtype", bold = "rbold")

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_column_style()
Per-cell styling targeting a single column.
set_column_style(
  x, column,
  bold = NULL, italic = NULL,
  color = NULL, bg = NULL,
  badge = NULL, icon = NULL
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget
required



column
character(1)
required
Field name of the target column.


bold / italic
column name
NULL
Logical-column driver.


color / bg
column name
NULL
CSS color column.


badge / icon
column name
NULL
Per-cell badge / icon.



When both row and column styles target the same cell, the column style wins.
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(col_pvalue("pval"))
) |>
  set_column_style("pval", bold = "is_sig", color = "sig_color")

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_marker_style()
Style the primary effect’s interval markers.
set_marker_style(x,
                 color = NULL, shape = NULL,
                 opacity = NULL, size = NULL)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




color
column name
NULL
CSS color column for marker fill.


shape
column name
NULL
Column of "square" / "circle" / "diamond" / "triangle".


opacity
column name
NULL
Numeric column 0–1.


size
column name
NULL
Numeric weight-like column (e.g. study weights 0–100). The renderer normalizes via 0.5 + sqrt(w/100) * 1.5. Pass raw weights, not a pre-normalized multiplier.



For multi-effect plots, additional effects use the per-effect color/shape/opacity from effect_forest().

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_zoom()
Zoom level and container constraints. Works on htmlwidgets and live tabviz_proxy objects (it is not a WebSpec modifier — apply it after rendering).
set_zoom(x,
         zoom = 1.0,
         auto_fit = TRUE,
         max_width = NULL,
         max_height = NULL,
         show_controls = TRUE)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
htmlwidget / tabviz_proxy
required



zoom
numeric(1)
1.0
0.5–2.0.


auto_fit
logical(1)
TRUE
Shrink content to fit container if too large; never enlarges.


max_width
numeric(1)
NULL
Max container width in px (≥ 100).


max_height
numeric(1)
NULL
Max container height in px (≥ 100).


show_controls
logical(1)
TRUE
Show zoom controls on hover.









ImportantReplaces tabviz() zoom arguments



tabviz(zoom = ...), tabviz(auto_fit = ...), tabviz(max_width = ...), tabviz(max_height = ...), and tabviz(show_zoom_controls = ...) were deprecated in 0.9.0. Pipe to set_zoom() after constructing the widget instead.


tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi"))) |>
  set_zoom(zoom = 0.8, max_width = 900)

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_theme()
Swap the active theme on a WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy. On a proxy, sends a message so the widget swaps themes without a full re-render.
set_theme(x, theme)








Argument
Type
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / tabviz_proxy



theme
character or WebTheme
Either a built-in name or a web_theme_*() object.



Built-in names: "default", "minimal", "dark", "jama", "lancet", "modern", "presentation", "cochrane", "nature".
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"))) |>
  set_theme("jama")

title: Styling modifiers
text:
selectable_themes()
Curate which themes appear in the in-widget theme switcher. This is the fluent sibling of the enable_themes argument of web_interaction().
selectable_themes(x, themes)








Argument
Type
Description




x
WebSpec or htmlwidget
Live proxy not supported.


themes
NULL / "default" / list of WebTheme
NULL hides the switcher; "default" shows all package_themes(); a list shows exactly those (named entries override displayed names). The active theme is always auto-included so the user can revert.



tabviz(data, label = "study") |>
  set_theme("jama") |>
  selectable_themes(list(
    Classical = web_theme_jama(),
    Modern    = web_theme_cochrane()
  ))

title: Styling modifiers
text:
Quick reference








Modifier
Operates on
Purpose




set_row_style()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Row-level styling and row types.


set_column_style()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Per-cell styling for a single column.


set_marker_style()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Marker color / shape / opacity / size driver.


set_zoom()
htmlwidget, tabviz_proxy
Zoom and container size constraints.


set_theme()
WebSpec, htmlwidget, tabviz_proxy
Swap the active theme.


selectable_themes()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Curate the in-widget theme switcher.
