title: Annotations
text: Annotations decorate a viz_forest() column with reference lines and custom shapes. They are passed via the annotations = list(...) argument of viz_forest() (and the equivalent shorthand in forest_plot()).

title: Annotations
text:
refline()
Vertical reference line at a specific x-axis position. Most often used to mark the null effect (x = 0 linear, x = 1 log) or a clinically meaningful threshold.
refline(x,
        label = NULL,
        style = c("dashed", "solid", "dotted"),
        color = NULL,
        width = 1,
        opacity = 0.6)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
numeric(1)
required
X-axis position.


label
character(1)
NULL
Text rendered near the line.


style
choice
"dashed"
"dashed", "solid", or "dotted".


color
character(1)
NULL
CSS color. NULL uses the theme default.


width
numeric(1)
1
Line width in px.


opacity
numeric(1)
0.6
0–1.



Returns: a ReferenceLine object.


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 1.15),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.90),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.45)
)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(1,    label = "No effect",            style = "solid"),
                 refline(0.75, label = "Clinically meaningful", style = "dashed")
               ))
  )
)

title: Annotations
text:
forest_annotation()
Custom shape attached to a specific study row.
forest_annotation(
  study_id,
  shape = c("circle", "square", "triangle", "star"),
  position = c("after", "before", "overlay"),
  color = "#8b5cf6",
  size = 1.0
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




study_id
character(1)
required
Identifier of the study to annotate.


shape
choice
"circle"
One of "circle", "square", "triangle", "star".


position
choice
"after"
Position relative to the point: "before", "after", or "overlay".


color
character(1)
"#8b5cf6"
CSS color.


size
numeric(1)
1.0
Size multiplier.



Returns: a CustomAnnotation object.






WarningFrontend rendering not implemented



forest_annotation() is currently a placeholder — the spec is built but the frontend has no renderer for it yet. Use refline() for visual annotations until this lands.

title: Annotations
text:
Related

viz_forest() — the annotations argument
forest_plot() — quick wrapper that forwards an annotations list

title: forest_plot()
text: forest_plot() is a convenience wrapper around tabviz() that auto-builds a forest column from point / lower / upper (or an effects list) and forwards everything else to tabviz(). Use it for quick forest plots; reach for tabviz() directly when you want full control over column ordering, multiple viz columns, or column groups.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Signature
forest_plot(
  x,
  point = NULL, lower = NULL, upper = NULL,
  effects = NULL,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  columns = NULL,
  scale = NULL,            # "linear" or "log"
  null_value = NULL,       # 0 (linear) or 1 (log) by default
  axis_label = NULL,
  axis_range = NULL,
  axis_ticks = NULL,
  axis_gridlines = NULL,
  theme = NULL,
  ...,                     # forwarded to tabviz()
  split_by = NULL,
  shared_axis = FALSE,
  width = NULL, height = NULL, elementId = NULL
)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Arguments



Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
data.frame / WebSpec / SplitForest
required
Input. A data frame builds a fresh widget; a WebSpec is rendered directly; a SplitForest is rendered as a split widget.


point
character(1)
NULL
Column name for point estimates. Required when x is a data frame and effects is not provided.


lower
character(1)
NULL
Lower-bound column. Required with point / upper.


upper
character(1)
NULL
Upper-bound column.


effects
list of effect_forest()
NULL
Multi-effect spec; when provided, point / lower / upper are ignored.


label
character(1)
NULL
Column name for row labels.


group
character / list
NULL
Same shape as tabviz(group=).


columns
list
NULL
Extra col_*() / viz_*() columns added alongside the auto-built forest column.


scale
character(1)
"linear"
"linear" or "log".


null_value
numeric(1)
0 (linear) / 1 (log)
Reference value for the null line.


axis_label
character(1)
"Effect"
X-axis label.


axis_range
numeric(2)
NULL
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


axis_ticks
numeric
NULL
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


axis_gridlines
logical(1)
FALSE
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


theme
WebTheme
NULL
Theme; NULL falls through to tabviz()’s default.


...
—
—
Forwarded to tabviz(). All tabviz() row/marker styling, titles, and interactivity arguments are accepted.


split_by
character
NULL
Splits the result into a SplitForest.


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
Share axis range across split sub-plots.


width, height, elementId
—
NULL
Standard htmlwidget passthroughs.









Noteaxis_range / axis_ticks / axis_gridlines are native here



In tabviz(), these arguments were deprecated in 0.9.0 and now live on viz_forest(). forest_plot() keeps them as native arguments and forwards them into the auto-built forest column — that’s the whole point of the wrapper.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Return value
An htmlwidget. When split_by is set or x is a SplitForest, returns the split-table widget.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Examples


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.68),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.52),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 0.89)
)

# Quick forest from a data frame
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study")








Code
# Log scale for ratios (HR / OR / RR)
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")








Code
# Add data columns alongside the auto-built forest column
data$n <- c(1250, 980, 1560, 1100)

forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1,
            columns = list(
              col_n("n"),
              col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
            ))








Code
# Multi-effect plot (e.g. ITT vs Per-Protocol)
multi <- data.frame(
  study  = c("TRIAL-1", "TRIAL-2", "TRIAL-3"),
  itt_hr = c(0.78, 0.82, 0.74),
  itt_lo = c(0.65, 0.68, 0.61),
  itt_hi = c(0.94, 0.99, 0.90),
  pp_hr  = c(0.72, 0.78, 0.70),
  pp_lo  = c(0.58, 0.63, 0.56),
  pp_hi  = c(0.89, 0.97, 0.88)
)

forest_plot(multi,
  label = "study",
  effects = list(
    effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", label = "ITT",          color = "#2563eb"),
    effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",  label = "Per-Protocol", color = "#16a34a")
  ),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)








Code
# Split view with shared axis
regional <- data.frame(
  study  = rep(c("Study 1", "Study 2", "Study 3"), 2),
  region = rep(c("North America", "Europe"), each = 3),
  hr     = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.78, 0.68, 0.82, 0.75),
  lo     = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.62, 0.52, 0.68, 0.58),
  hi     = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.98, 0.89, 0.99, 0.97)
)

forest_plot(regional, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1,
            split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Equivalence with tabviz()
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
            label = "study", scale = "log", null_value = 1)

# Equivalent to:
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Related

tabviz() — full control over columns and layout
viz_forest() — the underlying forest column helper
effect_forest() — define one effect for multi-effect plots
split_table() — build a SplitForest from a spec or widget
save_plot() — export to SVG/PNG/PDF

title: Data Operations
text:
The data-operation verbs provide a fluent, pipe-friendly interface for adding, removing, rearranging, filtering, sorting, and editing the columns, rows, and cells of a table. Every verb dispatches on input type:
This single surface means the same code can compose a static plot or drive a live Shiny widget.

title: Data Operations
text:
Column Operations

add_column()
Insert a new column into the table.
add_column(x, col, after = NULL)







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


col
A ColumnSpec from col_text(), col_numeric(), col_bar(), etc.


after
Field/id of an existing column to insert after. NULL (default) appends; "__start__" prepends.



Returns: Modified input (same type).


Code
spec <- web_spec(data, columns = list(col_text("study"), col_numeric("hr")))

# Append a column
spec |> add_column(col_numeric("se", "SE"))

# Insert after a specific field
spec |> add_column(col_badge("sig"), after = "hr")

# Prepend
spec |> add_column(col_icon("flag"), after = "__start__")




remove_column()
Remove a column by field or id.
remove_column(x, field)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column to remove



On a proxy this sends hideColumn, which hides the column client-side without destroying its spec — add_column() with the same field will restore it.


Code
spec |> remove_column("notes")




move_column()
Change a column’s position.
move_column(x, field, to)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column to move


to
1-based integer index, or the field name of another column



On the R side, indices are 1-based. The proxy payload converts to 0-based before sending to JS — you do not need to adjust.


Code
# Move to position 3
spec |> move_column("hr", to = 3L)

# Move before the "pval" column
spec |> move_column("hr", to = "pval")








Note



Within-group moves via static dispatch are currently unsupported — move_column() on a column that lives inside a col_group() will error. Use proxy dispatch or restructure groups.




resize_column()
Set a column’s pixel width.
resize_column(x, field, width)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column


width
Width in pixels (≥ 40)





Code
spec |> resize_column("study", width = 240)




update_column()
Edit properties of an existing column in place.
update_column(x, field, ...)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column


...
Named properties to update



Top-level slots written directly: header, align, header_align, wrap, sortable, width. Any other name is merged into the column’s options list (useful for type-specific knobs such as decimals, format, colors).


Code
spec |>
  update_column("hr",
    header = "Hazard Ratio",
    align = "right",
    decimals = 3
  )

title: Data Operations
text:
Row & Data Operations

sort_rows()
Order rows by a column’s values.
sort_rows(x, by, direction = c("asc", "desc", "none"))







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


by
Field or id of the column to sort by


direction
"asc", "desc", or "none" (clears sort on a proxy)



On static inputs, direction = "none" is a no-op; the spec data frame keeps its existing order.


Code
spec |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "desc")




filter_rows()
Keep rows that match a predicate.
filter_rows(x, field, operator = c("eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains"), value)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field to filter on


operator
"eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", or "contains"


value
Scalar value to compare against





Code
spec |> filter_rows("region", "eq", "North America")
spec |> filter_rows("hr", "lt", 1)


On static inputs, rows where the predicate is NA are dropped.


clear_filters()
Remove any runtime filters.
clear_filters(x)
No-op for static inputs (there is no persisted filter state). On a proxy, sends clearFilter to reset the widget’s filter stack.


select_rows()
Replace the current row selection (proxy only).
select_rows(x, row_ids)



Argument
Description




x
A tabviz_proxy


row_ids
Character vector of row ids to select



On static inputs, warns and returns the input unchanged — selection is a runtime concept with no spec-level representation.


Code
observeEvent(input$highlight, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  select_rows(proxy, c("Trial A", "Trial C"))
})




move_row()
Reposition a row within the data frame.
move_row(x, row_id, to)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


row_id
Row id (the value in the leftmost label/id column)


to
1-based integer index, or another row id





Code
spec |> move_row("Summary", to = 1L)




update_data()
Replace the entire underlying data frame.
update_data(x, data)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


data
A data frame (static inputs) or WebSpec (proxy)



For a proxy you must pass a complete WebSpec, not a raw data frame — column specs, themes, and annotations need to be re-serialized together. Build a fresh spec with tabviz() or web_spec() and pass it in.


Code
# Static: swap the data frame
spec |> update_data(new_df)

# Proxy: full re-specification
observeEvent(input$refresh, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  fresh <- tabviz(reactive_data(), label = "study",
                  columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")))
  update_data(proxy, attr(fresh, "webspec"))
})

title: Data Operations
text:
Group Operations

toggle_group()
Collapse or expand a named group.
toggle_group(x, group_id, collapsed = NULL)







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


group_id
Id of the group to toggle


collapsed
TRUE to collapse, FALSE to expand, NULL to flip current state



Groups are defined via the group argument of tabviz(); if a spec has none, toggle_group() warns for static inputs.


Code
spec |> toggle_group("secondary_endpoints", collapsed = TRUE)

title: Data Operations
text:
Cell Edits (Proxy Only)
These verbs carry changes directly into a running widget. They have no spec-level counterpart — on static inputs they warn and return unchanged.

set_cell()
Overwrite a single cell.
set_cell(x, row_id, field, value)


Code
set_cell(proxy, row_id = "Trial A", field = "hr", value = 0.84)




set_row_label()
Rename a row’s label.
set_row_label(x, row_id, label)


Code
set_row_label(proxy, "Trial A", "Trial A (updated)")




clear_edits()
Discard every in-widget cell and label edit applied since the last full render.
clear_edits(x)

title: Data Operations
text:
Global controls
set_theme() and set_zoom() are documented in the styling reference — they are global presentation modifiers rather than data verbs, but they accept the same input shapes (spec / widget / proxy where applicable).

title: Data Operations
text:
Verb Reference

Columns



Function
Purpose




add_column()
Insert a new column


remove_column()
Remove a column (proxy: hide)


move_column()
Change column position


resize_column()
Set pixel width


update_column()
Edit header, alignment, options





Rows & data



Function
Purpose




sort_rows()
Order rows by a column


filter_rows()
Keep rows matching a predicate


clear_filters()
Clear runtime filters (proxy)


select_rows()
Select rows (proxy)


move_row()
Reposition a row


update_data()
Replace the data frame





Groups & cells



Function
Purpose




toggle_group()
Collapse/expand a group


set_cell()
Overwrite a cell (proxy)


set_row_label()
Rename a row (proxy)


clear_edits()
Discard in-widget edits (proxy)

title: Data Operations
text:
See Also

Styling functions
Fluent API guide
Shiny integration

title: Troubleshooting
text: This guide covers common issues and their solutions when using tabviz.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Quick Diagnostic Checklist
When something isn’t working, check:

Column names — Are they spelled correctly and exist in the data?
Data types — Are numeric columns actually numeric?
NAs — Are there unexpected missing values?
Scale — Using scale = "log" with non-positive values?
Dependencies — Is V8 installed (always required) and rsvg (for PNG/PDF)?
Deprecated arguments — Did 0.9.0 rename what you’re passing? See Recent renames.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Data Issues

“Column not found in data”
Symptom: Error message about missing column.
Cause: Column name typo or the column doesn’t exist.
Solution:


Code
# Check what columns exist
names(your_data)

# Common mistake: using wrong case
forest_plot(data, point = "HR", ...)  # Wrong: "HR"
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", ...)  # Correct: "hr"




Unexpected NAs in Plot
Symptom: Rows appear without intervals or with missing values.
Cause: Columns contain NA values or wrong data types.
Solution:


Code
# Check for NAs
summary(your_data)

# Check column types - character columns won't work for numeric fields
sapply(your_data, class)

# Fix character columns that should be numeric
your_data$hr <- as.numeric(your_data$hr)




Groups Not Displaying Correctly
Symptom: Hierarchical groups appear flat or have wrong nesting.
Cause: Group column contains unexpected values or ID collisions.
Solution:


Code
# Check unique group values
table(your_data$region)
table(your_data$subregion)

# Ensure nested groups have consistent parent values
your_data |>
  group_by(region, subregion) |>
  count()




Data Rows Mixed with Headers
Symptom: Header rows show intervals, or data rows appear as headers.
Cause: row_type column has incorrect values.
Solution:


Code
# Valid row_type values
valid_types <- c("header", "data", "summary", "spacer")

# Check your values
your_data |>
  count(row_type)

# Ensure header/spacer rows have NA for estimates
your_data <- your_data |>
  mutate(
    hr = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, hr),
    lower = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, lower),
    upper = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, upper)
  )

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Scale and Axis Issues

Log Scale with Non-Positive Values
Symptom: Error or unexpected axis range when using scale = "log".
Cause: Data contains zero or negative values, which are undefined on log scale.
Solution:


Code
# Find problematic rows
your_data |>
  filter(hr <= 0 | lower <= 0 | upper <= 0)

# Option 1: Filter out invalid rows
valid_data <- your_data |>
  filter(hr > 0, lower > 0, upper > 0)

# Option 2: Use linear scale instead
forest_plot(your_data, ..., scale = "linear", null_value = 0)




Axis Range Too Wide
Symptom: Data points are compressed into a small portion of the axis.
Cause: Outliers or automatic axis calculation including extreme values.
Solution:


Code
# Check data range
range(your_data$hr, na.rm = TRUE)
range(your_data$lower, na.rm = TRUE)
range(your_data$upper, na.rm = TRUE)

# Option 1: Override axis range
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  axis_range = c(0.5, 2.0)  # Focus on relevant range
)

# Option 2: Filter outliers
filtered <- your_data |>
  filter(between(hr, 0.3, 3))




Axis Range Too Narrow
Symptom: Confidence intervals extend beyond axis bounds.
Cause: Manual axis range doesn’t accommodate all data.
Solution:


Code
# Find the actual data extent
min_val <- min(your_data$lower, na.rm = TRUE)
max_val <- max(your_data$upper, na.rm = TRUE)
message("Data range: ", round(min_val, 2), " to ", round(max_val, 2))

# Adjust axis_range to include all data
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  axis_range = c(min_val * 0.9, max_val * 1.1)
)




Tick Marks Not Appearing
Symptom: Axis shows but has no tick marks or unexpected tick positions.
Solution:


Code
# Specify explicit tick positions
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  scale = "log",
  axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 2)  # Explicit ticks
)

# Or use theme settings
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "tick_count"), 5) |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
)








NoteOn tabviz() (vs forest_plot())



tabviz() no longer takes axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines (deprecated in 0.9.0). Pass them to viz_forest() on the forest column instead:
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.5, 2.0),
               axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 2))
  )
)
forest_plot() keeps these as native top-level arguments and forwards them to the auto-built forest column.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Export Failures

V8 Not Installed
Symptom: Error when calling save_plot(): “Package V8 is required”
Solution:


Code
# Install V8
install.packages("V8")

# On some systems, you may need system libraries first:
# macOS: brew install v8
# Ubuntu: sudo apt-get install libv8-dev




rsvg Issues
Symptom: PDF/PNG export fails with rsvg errors.
Solution:


Code
# Install rsvg
install.packages("rsvg")

# On macOS, you may need:
# brew install librsvg

# On Ubuntu:
# sudo apt-get install librsvg2-dev




“SVG generator JavaScript file not found”
Symptom: Export fails with message about missing JS file.
Cause: Package installation is incomplete.
Solution:


Code
# Reinstall the package
pak::pak("kaskarn/tabviz", force = TRUE)

# Verify installation
system.file("js/svg-generator.js", package = "tabviz")
# Should return a path, not ""




Colors Differ Between Display and Export
Symptom: Exported SVG/PNG has different colors than the interactive widget.
Cause: Theme not applied consistently to both.
Solution:


Code
# Use the same theme for display and export
my_theme <- web_theme_lancet()

# Interactive
forest_plot(data, ..., theme = my_theme)

# Export
spec <- web_spec(data, ..., theme = my_theme)
save_plot(spec, "output.svg")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Widget Rendering Issues

Blank Output in RStudio Viewer
Symptom: Plot doesn’t appear in RStudio’s Viewer pane.
Cause: Viewer pane size or rendering issues.
Solution:


Code
# Try in browser instead
plot <- forest_plot(your_data, ...)
htmltools::browsable(plot)

# Or save to file and open
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(plot, "temp.html")
browseURL("temp.html")




Widget Not Sizing Correctly
Symptom: Plot is too small, cut off, or doesn’t fill container.
Solution:


Code
# Specify explicit dimensions
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  width = 900,
  height = 600
)

# For Shiny, use CSS units
tabvizOutput("plot", width = "100%", height = "600px")

# Constrain max size via set_zoom() (replaces deprecated tabviz(max_width=)/(max_height=))
tabviz(your_data, ...) |>
  set_zoom(max_width = 1000, auto_fit = TRUE)




Shiny: Proxy Not Working
Symptom: tabviz_proxy() calls don’t update the plot.
Cause: Proxy created outside reactive context.
Solution:


Code
# Wrong: proxy at top level
# proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")  # This will fail!

# Correct: proxy inside reactive context
observeEvent(input$button, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  sort_rows(proxy, "hr", "asc")
})

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Theme and Styling Issues

Styles Not Applying
Symptom: Bold, color, or other styles don’t appear.
Cause: Passing values directly instead of column names.
Solution:


Code
# Wrong: passing values directly
col_numeric("hr", bold = TRUE, color = "#ff0000")

# Correct: passing column names that contain the values
col_numeric("hr", bold = "is_bold", color = "hr_color")

# The data must have these columns:
data <- data |>
  mutate(
    is_bold = pval < 0.05,
    hr_color = if_else(hr < 1, "#16a34a", "#dc2626")
  )




Font Not Loading
Symptom: Text appears in wrong font.
Cause: Custom font not available on system.
Solution:


Code
# Use web-safe fallback fonts
web_theme_cochrane() |>
  set_inputs(font_body = "Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif")

# For exports, fonts must be installed on the rendering system

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Debug Patterns

Inspecting a WebSpec


Code
# Build a spec without rendering (use .spec_only = TRUE on tabviz())
spec <- tabviz(
  data.frame(
    study = c("A", "B"),
    hr = c(0.8, 1.2),
    n = c(100, 200)
  ),
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
    col_n("n")
  ),
  .spec_only = TRUE
)

# Print summary
print(spec)


<tabviz::WebSpec>
 @ data              :'data.frame': 2 obs. of  3 variables:
 .. $ study: chr  "A" "B"
 .. $ hr   : num  0.8 1.2
 .. $ n    : num  100 200
 @ group_col         : chr NA
 @ group_cols        : chr(0) 
 @ columns           :List of 3
 .. $ : <tabviz::ColumnSpec>
 ..  ..@ id            : chr "label"
 ..  ..@ header        : chr "Study"
 ..  ..@ field         : chr "study"
 ..  ..@ type          : chr "text"
 ..  ..@ width         : chr "auto"
 ..  ..@ align         : chr "left"
 ..  ..@ header_align  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ show_header   : logi NA
 ..  ..@ wrap          : logi FALSE
 ..  ..@ sortable      : logi TRUE
 ..  ..@ options       : list()
 ..  ..@ style_bold    : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_italic  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_color   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_bg      : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_badge   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_icon    : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_emphasis: chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_muted   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_accent  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_tooltip : NULL
 ..  ..@ formatter     : NULL
 .. $ : <tabviz::ColumnSpec>
 ..  ..@ id            : chr "numeric_hr"
 ..  ..@ header        : chr "HR"
 ..  ..@ field         : chr "hr"
 ..  ..@ type          : chr "numeric"
 ..  ..@ width         : chr "auto"
 ..  ..@ align         : chr "left"
 ..  ..@ header_align  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ show_header   : logi NA
 ..  ..@ wrap          : logi FALSE
 ..  ..@ sortable      : logi TRUE
 ..  ..@ options       :List of 1
 .. .. .. $ numeric:List of 4
 .. .. ..  ..$ decimals    : num 2
 .. .. ..  ..$ digits      : NULL
 .. .. ..  ..$ thousandsSep: logi FALSE
 .. .. ..  ..$ abbreviate  : logi FALSE
 ..  ..@ style_bold    : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_italic  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_color   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_bg      : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_badge   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_icon    : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_emphasis: chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_muted   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_accent  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_tooltip : NULL
 ..  ..@ formatter     : NULL
 .. $ : <tabviz::ColumnSpec>
 ..  ..@ id            : chr "numeric_n"
 ..  ..@ header        : chr "N"
 ..  ..@ field         : chr "n"
 ..  ..@ type          : chr "numeric"
 ..  ..@ width         : chr "auto"
 ..  ..@ align         : chr "left"
 ..  ..@ header_align  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ show_header   : logi NA
 ..  ..@ wrap          : logi FALSE
 ..  ..@ sortable      : logi TRUE
 ..  ..@ options       :List of 1
 .. .. .. $ numeric:List of 4
 .. .. ..  ..$ decimals    : num 0
 .. .. ..  ..$ digits      : NULL
 .. .. ..  ..$ thousandsSep: chr ","
 .. .. ..  ..$ abbreviate  : logi FALSE
 ..  ..@ style_bold    : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_italic  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_color   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_bg      : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_badge   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_icon    : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_emphasis: chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_muted   : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_accent  : chr NA
 ..  ..@ style_tooltip : NULL
 ..  ..@ formatter     : NULL
 @ extra_columns     : list()

Minimal Reproducible Example
When reporting issues, create a minimal example:


Code
# Minimal data that reproduces the problem
test_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Test 1", "Test 2"),
  hr = c(0.8, 1.2),
  lower = c(0.6, 0.9),
  upper = c(1.1, 1.6)
)

# Minimal code
forest_plot(test_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study"
)

# Include session info
sessionInfo()

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Recent renames
The 0.9.0 release renamed several arguments. The old names still work for one cycle and emit lifecycle::deprecate_warn() — but if you’re hitting a “no such argument” error, check this table:








Old
New
Where




col_interval(..., sep = " ")
separator = " "
col_interval()


col_reference(..., icon = TRUE)
show_icon = TRUE
col_reference()


col_n(), col_bar(), col_pvalue(), col_sparkline() with no field
Pass field explicitly
col_*()


set_colors(), set_typography(), set_shapes(), set_axis(), set_layout(), set_group_headers(), set_effect_colors(), set_marker_shapes()
set_inputs(), set_variants(), set_spacing(), set_theme_field(theme, path, value)
v2 modifier surface (theme rewrite)


web_interaction(show_filters = ...)
enable_filters = ...
web_interaction()


tabviz(axis_range=, axis_ticks=, axis_gridlines=)
Pass to viz_forest() on the forest column
tabviz()


tabviz(zoom=, auto_fit=, max_width=, max_height=, show_zoom_controls=)
Pipe to set_zoom() after constructing the widget
tabviz()


tabviz(weight = "col")
marker_size = "col"
tabviz()



forest_plot() still accepts axis_range, axis_ticks, and axis_gridlines natively — those were only deprecated on tabviz().

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Getting Help
If you’ve tried the above and still have issues:

Check GitHub Issues for similar problems
Create a minimal reproducible example
Include sessionInfo() output
Open a new issue with your example and session info

title: Function Reference
text: Complete reference for the tabviz public API. Functions are grouped by purpose; each name links to its detail page.

title: Function Reference
text:
Entry points



Function
Description




tabviz()
Main entry point — interactive table with any mix of columns.


forest_plot()
Convenience wrapper that auto-builds a forest column from point / lower / upper (or effects).


web_spec()
Alias of tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE) — return a WebSpec instead of a widget.


split_table()
Build a SplitForest from a spec / widget / data frame.



# Build a table with a forest plot column
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
  col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
  col_pvalue("pval")
))

# Quick forest plot
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi", label = "study")

title: Function Reference
text:
Troubleshooting
Troubleshooting guide — common errors and the recent (0.9.0) renames.

title: Export
text:
Export tabviz widgets to static file formats (SVG, PDF, PNG).
The two exported entry points are:
Both delegate to a shared SVG generator (the same one the browser uses), then convert to PDF / PNG via rsvg. See save_plot() for the full reference, dependencies, and resolution guidance.
Code
p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg")
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png", width = 1200, scale = 3)
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.pdf", width = 800)

title: Export
text:
Related

save_plot() — the single-plot exporter and save_split_table() for split views
split_table() — produce the SplitForest accepted by save_split_table()
Themes — theme presets aimed at print (web_theme_jama(), web_theme_lancet(), web_theme_cochrane())
web_interaction_publication() — quiet, print-clean interaction preset

title: S7 Class Architecture
text: A reference catalog of every S7 class in tabviz, with the slot names you’ll actually see if you peek inside a WebSpec. Read this when you need to extend the package, debug serialization, or understand why a particular argument resolves the way it does.

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Overview
tabviz uses S7, R’s modern object-oriented system, for its internal data structures. S7 provides:

Type safety — Properties are validated at construction time
Composition — Complex objects built from simpler components
Method dispatch — Generic functions that work across types
Immutability by default — Modifications create new objects

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          WebSpec                                 │
│  The root specification object passed to tabviz()               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  data          │ data.frame with point/interval values          │
│  columns       │ List<ColumnSpec | ColumnGroup>                 │
│  groups        │ List<GroupSpec>                                │
│  theme         │ WebTheme                                       │
│  interaction   │ InteractionSpec                                │
│  annotations   │ List<Annotation>                               │
│  ...           │ Row styling column mappings                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         ├──────────────────────────┐
         │                          │
         ▼                          ▼
┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────────────┐
│    WebTheme     │      │      ColumnSpec/Group       │
├─────────────────┤      ├─────────────────────────────┤
│ colors          │      │ id, header, field, type     │
│ typography      │      │ width, align, position      │
│ spacing         │      │ style mappings              │
│ shapes          │      └─────────────────────────────┘
│ axis            │
│ layout          │
└─────────────────┘

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Class Hierarchy

Core Classes (R/classes-core.R)







Class
Purpose




WebSpec
Root specification containing all data and configuration


GroupSpec
Row grouping definition with optional nesting


EffectSpec
Multi-effect plot configuration (multiple intervals per row)


GroupSummary
Aggregate statistics for a group


PlotLabels
Title, subtitle, caption, footnote text


Heterogeneity
Meta-analysis heterogeneity statistics (optional)





Theme Classes (R/classes-theme.R)
The v2 theme is a 3-tier S7 hierarchy. Tier 1 holds customer-facing inputs and per-table variants; Tier 2 holds derived semantic role tokens (chrome and data, parallel hierarchies); Tier 3 holds component bindings consumed by the renderer.








Tier
Class
Purpose




1
ThemeInputs
Customer-facing palette + fonts + neutrals


1
ThemeVariants
Per-table toggles (density, header_style, first_column_style)


2 chrome
Surfaces
base / muted / raised


2 chrome
Content
primary / secondary / muted / inverse


2 chrome
Dividers
subtle / strong


2 chrome
AccentRoles
default / muted / tint_subtle / tint_medium


2 chrome
StatusColors
positive / negative / warning / info


2 data
SlotBundle
7-field bundle (used in series list and summary)


2 type
TextRole, TextRoles
Per-role typography bundles (8 named roles)


2 layout
SpacingTokens
12 density-derived numerics


3
AnnotationCluster
title / subtitle / caption / footnote


3
HeaderCluster, HeaderVariant
Light + bold variants of the column-header band


3
ColumnGroupCluster
Column-group header band


3
RowGroupCluster, RowGroupTier
L1 / L2 / L3 nested row-group hierarchy


3
RowCluster, RowState, RowSemantic
Row states (default/alt/hover/selected) + semantic bundles (emphasis/muted/accent) + banding


3
CellCluster
Cell bg / fg / border / text


3
FirstColumnCluster, FirstColumnVariant
Plain + bold variants of the row-label column


3
PlotScaffold
Axis line / tick / gridline / reference + mark sizes


3
MarksRecipes, MarkRecipe
Per-mark slot-bundle wiring


config
AxisConfig
Forest x-axis range / ticks / gridlines


config
Layout
Plot width, container border


top
WebTheme
Composes all of the above



Hard rule: Tier 2 chrome derivation reads only chrome inputs (neutral, brand, brand_deep, accent, accent_deep). Tier 2 data derivation reads only data inputs (series_anchors, status_*) plus surface.base for muted blends. The two cascades meet only at WebTheme reassembly.


Component Classes (R/classes-components.R)







Class
Purpose




ColumnSpec
Single column definition


ColumnGroup
Hierarchical column header grouping


InteractionSpec
UI interaction toggles (sort, collapse, select, etc.)





Annotation Classes (R/classes-annotations.R)



Class
Purpose




ReferenceLine
Vertical reference line at specific x value


CustomAnnotation
Shape annotation on specific rows


RiskOfBias
Cochrane-style risk of bias traffic lights

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Design Patterns

1. Class + Helper Function Pattern
Every S7 class has a corresponding helper function with a user-friendly API:
# S7 class (internal)
GroupSpec <- new_class("GroupSpec", properties = list(
  id = class_character,
  label = class_character,
  collapsed = new_property(class_logical, default = FALSE),
  parent_id = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
))

# Helper function (user-facing)
web_group <- function(id, label = id, parent = NULL, collapsed = FALSE) {
  GroupSpec(
    id = as.character(id),
    label = as.character(label),
    collapsed = collapsed,
    parent_id = if (is.null(parent)) NA_character_ else as.character(parent)
  )
}
This pattern provides:

Type coercion — Helper handles NULL → NA conversion
Defaults — Sensible defaults without cluttering class definition
Documentation — roxygen docs on the helper, not the class
Validation — Additional business logic before construction



2. Composition over inheritance, with explicit tiers
WebTheme composes ~20 sub-classes organized into three tiers. Tier 1 is authored; Tier 2 is derived from Tier 1 by resolve_theme(); Tier 3 is component-binding leaves derived from Tier 2.
WebTheme <- new_class("WebTheme", properties = list(
  name     = new_property(class_character, default = "default"),
  inputs   = new_property(ThemeInputs,    default = ThemeInputs()),
  variants = new_property(ThemeVariants,  default = ThemeVariants()),

  # Tier 2 (derived; NA-default until resolve_theme runs)
  surface = new_property(Surfaces,      default = Surfaces()),
  content = new_property(Content,       default = Content()),
  divider = new_property(Dividers,      default = Dividers()),
  accent  = new_property(AccentRoles,   default = AccentRoles()),
  status  = new_property(StatusColors,  default = StatusColors()),
  series  = new_property(class_list,    default = list()),
  summary = new_property(SlotBundle,    default = SlotBundle()),
  text    = new_property(TextRoles,     default = TextRoles()),
  spacing = new_property(SpacingTokens, default = SpacingTokens()),

  # Tier 3 (component bindings)
  annotation   = new_property(AnnotationCluster, ...),
  header       = new_property(HeaderCluster,     ...),
  column_group = new_property(ColumnGroupCluster, ...),
  row_group    = new_property(RowGroupCluster,   ...),
  row          = new_property(RowCluster,        ...),
  cell         = new_property(CellCluster,       ...),
  first_column = new_property(FirstColumnCluster, ...),
  plot         = new_property(PlotScaffold,      ...),
  marks        = new_property(MarksRecipes,      ...),

  # Config (not part of the tier cascade)
  axis   = new_property(AxisConfig, default = AxisConfig()),
  layout = new_property(Layout,     default = Layout())
))


3. Fluent modifiers
Theme modification uses pipe-friendly functions that return re-resolved copies:
web_theme_jama() |>
  set_inputs(brand = "#0066cc") |>
  set_variants(density = "comfortable") |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 24) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
Implementation pattern (simplified):
set_inputs <- function(theme, ...) {
  stopifnot(S7_inherits(theme, WebTheme))
  args <- list(...)
  for (prop in names(args)) {
    S7::prop(theme@inputs, prop) <- args[[prop]]
  }
  resolve_theme(theme)
}
Each modifier re-runs resolve_theme() before returning. Resolution is idempotent and only fills NA-valued fields, so explicit user overrides survive.


4. NA vs NULL Convention
The package follows a consistent pattern for optional values:








Type
Default
Meaning




Scalar optional (string, number)
NA_character_ / NA_real_
“Not specified”


Complex object optional
NULL
“Not present”



Examples:
# Scalar optional — use NA
label_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
weight_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)

# Complex object optional — use NULL
overall_summary = new_property(
  new_union(GroupSummary, class_missing),
  default = NULL
)
theme = new_property(class_any, default = NULL)
This is checked during serialization:
# NA → NULL for JSON
label = if (is.na(spec@label_col)) NULL else spec@label_col


5. Validators for Business Logic
S7 validators enforce invariants that can’t be expressed through types alone. The real WebSpec validator just checks optional-column references resolve; stronger checks (log-scale positivity, lower <= upper) are done upstream in tabviz() and in viz_forest() / ColumnSpec construction where the column-to-data binding is known.
WebSpec <- new_class("WebSpec",
  properties = list(...),
  validator = function(self) {
    cols <- names(self@data)
    if (!is.na(self@group_col) && !self@group_col %in% cols) {
      return(paste0("Column '", self@group_col, "' not found in data"))
    }
    NULL  # Return NULL if valid
  }
)

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
WebSpec Deep Dive
WebSpec is the central data structure. Understanding it is key to understanding the package.

Core Data Properties
WebSpec <- new_class("WebSpec", properties = list(
  # Source data
  data = class_data.frame,

  # Group column mappings
  group_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  group_cols = new_property(class_character, default = character(0)),

  # Column specifications (a list of ColumnSpec / ColumnGroup; viz_forest()
  # owns its own point/lower/upper/scale/null_value)
  columns = new_property(class_list, default = list()),
  extra_columns = new_property(class_list, default = list()),
  available_exclude = new_property(class_character, default = character(0)),

  # Marker styling column mappings
  marker_color_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  marker_shape_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  marker_opacity_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  marker_size_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  weight_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),  # deprecated alias

  # Configuration
  theme = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  interaction = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  labels = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  ...
))
The forest column’s point / lower / upper / scale / null_value are properties of the viz_forest() ColumnSpec, not of WebSpec. The same is true for viz_bar(), viz_boxplot(), viz_violin() — each carries its own data references inside ColumnSpec@options.


Row Styling Column Mappings
Row-level styling is controlled via column references, not inline values:
# Instead of storing style values directly:
# row_bold = c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)  ← NOT this

# We store the column name containing the values:
row_bold_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_italic_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_color_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_bg_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_badge_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_icon_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_indent_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_type_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
marker_size_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
Usage in tabviz():
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = "is_summary",        # Column name, not values
  row_badge = "significance",     # Column name
  marker_size = "study_weight"    # Column name (formerly `weight`)
)
This pattern:

Keeps data and configuration separate
Allows dynamic styling from data columns
Avoids duplicating data in the spec



Hierarchical Groups
Groups support nesting via parent_id:
GroupSpec(id = "north_america", label = "North America", parent_id = NA_character_)
GroupSpec(id = "usa", label = "United States", parent_id = "north_america")
GroupSpec(id = "canada", label = "Canada", parent_id = "north_america")
The group_cols property tracks hierarchy for composite ID generation:
# For data grouped by region > country
group_cols = c("region", "country")  # Outer to inner
group_col = "country"                # Deepest level for row assignment

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
ColumnSpec Design
Columns support multiple types with type-specific options:
ColumnSpec <- new_class("ColumnSpec", properties = list(
  id = class_character,
  header = class_character,
  field = class_character,
  type = new_property(class_character, default = "text"),
  width = new_property(class_any, default = NA_real_),  # numeric or "auto"
  align = new_property(class_character, default = "left"),
  options = new_property(class_list, default = list()),

  # Per-cell style mappings
  style_bold = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_italic = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_color = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_bg = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_badge = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_icon = new_property(class_any, default = NULL)
))

Type-Specific Options
The options property holds type-specific configuration:
# Bar column
col_bar("weight", max_value = 100, show_label = TRUE, color = "#3b82f6")
# Creates: options = list(bar = list(maxValue = 100, showLabel = TRUE, color = "#3b82f6"))

# P-value column
col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE, thresholds = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001))
# Creates: options = list(pvalue = list(stars = TRUE, thresholds = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001)))

# Sparkline column
col_sparkline("trend", type = "area", height = 24)
# Creates: options = list(sparkline = list(type = "area", height = 24))


ColumnGroup for Hierarchical Headers
ColumnGroup <- new_class("ColumnGroup", properties = list(
  id = class_character,
  header = class_character,
  columns = new_property(class_list, default = list())
))
Usage:
col_group("Results",
  col_interval("or", "lo", "hi", header = "OR (95% CI)"),
  col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
)

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Serialization to JavaScript
S7 objects are converted to JSON for the JavaScript frontend via serialize_spec():
R (S7 objects)                    JSON                      TypeScript
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WebSpec                    →    { data: {...},        →    WebSpec interface
  @data                         columns: [...],
  @columns                      theme: {...},
  @theme                        ... }
  ...

WebTheme                   →    { name: "...",        →    WebTheme interface
  @colors                       colors: {...},
  @typography                   typography: {...},
  ...                           ... }
Key transformations:



R Pattern
JSON Pattern




snake_case property
camelCase key


NA_character_
null


NA_real_
null


S7 nested object
Nested JSON object


class_list of S7
Array of JSON objects



Example serialization:
serialize_theme <- function(theme) {
  list(
    name = theme@name,
    colors = list(
      background = theme@colors@background,
      foreground = theme@colors@foreground,
      # snake_case → camelCase
      intervalPositive = theme@colors@interval_positive,
      intervalNegative = theme@colors@interval_negative,
      ...
    ),
    ...
  )
}

title: S7 Class Architecture
S7 Class Architecture
S7 Class Architecture
text:
Best Practices

Creating New Classes

Start with the helper function API, then design the class to support it
Use NA_* for optional scalars, NULL for optional objects
Add validators for invariants that can’t be expressed as types
Document the helper, not the class directly



Modifying Existing Classes

Add new properties with defaults to maintain backward compatibility
Update serialization in utils-serialize.R
Update TypeScript types in srcjs/src/types/index.ts
Update fluent modifiers if the property should be user-configurable



Testing Classes
# Test construction
spec <- web_spec(df, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")
expect_s7_class(spec, WebSpec)

# Test validation
expect_error(
  WebSpec(data = df, point_col = "nonexistent", ...),
  "not found in data"
)

# Test serialization round-trip
json <- serialize_spec(spec)
expect_equal(json$data$pointCol, "hr")

title: Recipes
text: Short, self-contained snippets for specific tasks. Each recipe is a single pattern you can lift into your own code. For broader patterns see the Fluent API guide; for full parameter references see the reference section.

title: Recipes
text:
Color rows by significance threshold
Bold + green for p < 0.05, muted gray otherwise.


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                            null_value = 1, scale = "log")),
  row_bold  = ~ pval < 0.05,
  row_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.05, "#166534", "#64748b")
)

title: Recipes
text:
Badge the top-N rows
Rank by weight, tag the top 3.


Code
data |>
  mutate(
    rank  = rank(-weight),
    badge = ifelse(rank <= 3, paste0("#", rank), NA_character_)
  ) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                            null_value = 1, scale = "log")),
    row_badge = "badge"
  )

title: Recipes
Recipes
section: Filter via a slider in Shiny

title: Recipes
text:
Format p-values with thresholds
Switch to scientific notation for very small values, abbreviate below a cutoff, append significance stars.


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_pvalue("pval", "P",
               exp_threshold    = 0.001,   # scientific notation below this
               abbrev_threshold = 1e-4,    # show "<0.0001" below this
               stars            = TRUE)    # append * / ** / *** by `thresholds`
  )
)

title: Recipes
section: Pin an annotation line at the null value
text:
Pin an annotation line at the null value
Use refline() in viz_forest()’s annotations list.


Code
viz_forest(
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1,
  annotations = list(
    refline(1,    label = "No effect",  style = "solid",  color = "#111"),
    refline(0.85, label = "Pooled HR", style = "dashed", color = "#0066cc")
  )
)

title: Recipes
section: Split by subgroup with a shared axis
text:
Split by subgroup with a shared axis
Hierarchical navigation; every pane uses the same scale.


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                            null_value = 1, scale = "log")),
  split_by     = c("region", "country"),
  shared_axis  = TRUE
)

title: Recipes
section: Export a static plot at 2× resolution
text:
Export a static plot at 2× resolution
scale controls the pixel density of raster output.


Code
plot <- tabviz(data, ...)

save_plot(plot, "forest.svg")                    # vector
save_plot(plot, "forest.png", scale = 2)         # retina PNG
save_plot(plot, "forest.pdf", width = 8, height = 6)

title: Recipes
section: Build a table-only view (no forest column)
text:
Build a table-only view (no forest column)
Drop viz_forest() — any table works without a visualization column.


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pval", "P")
  )
)

title: Recipes
text:
Show multiple effect sizes side-by-side
Stack viz_forest() columns with a shared axis range.


Code
axis_range <- c(0.4, 2.0)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr_primary", lower = "primary_lo", upper = "primary_hi",
               header = "Primary outcome",
               axis_range = axis_range, null_value = 1, scale = "log"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr_secondary", lower = "secondary_lo", upper = "secondary_hi",
               header = "Secondary outcome",
               axis_range = axis_range, null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  )
)

title: Quick Start
text: Build your first interactive forest plot in about 30 lines, then layer in styling, themes, and conditional formatting.

title: Quick Start
text:
Installation
# Install from GitHub using pak
install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("kaskarn/tabviz")






NoteKey concepts




Label column: the row identifier (study name, category, item).
Column types: col_*() for data columns, viz_*() for visualizations.
Row types: "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".
Column mapping: pass column names as strings; values are computed per row.

title: Quick Start
text:
Your first table


Code
data(glp1_trials)

meta_data <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5)

tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)












TipBuilt-in datasets



This example uses glp1_trials, one of five datasets shipped with the package. See Package Data for all available datasets.

title: Quick Start
text:
The core pattern
tabviz(data,
  label = "label_column",    # row labels
  columns = list(...)        # what columns to show
)
Each col_*() function defines a column. The most common helpers:








Function
Purpose
Example




col_text()
Text
col_text("category")


col_numeric()
Numbers
col_numeric("value", decimals = 1)


col_n()
Sample sizes
col_n("n")


viz_forest()
Forest plot
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")


col_interval()
Point (CI)
col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR")


col_pvalue()
P-values
col_pvalue("pval")



See Columns for all 16+ column types.

title: Quick Start
text:
Adding more columns


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_numeric("events", header = "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)

title: Quick Start
text:
Applying themes
tabviz includes 9 publication-ready themes:

Code
tabviz(meta_data[1:3, ],
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama(), title = "JAMA"
)
tabviz(meta_data[1:3, ],
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_lancet(), title = "Lancet"
)













Available themes: web_theme_cochrane(), web_theme_jama(), web_theme_lancet(), web_theme_cochrane(), web_theme_cochrane(), web_theme_cochrane(), web_theme_cochrane() |> set_variants(density = "spacious"), web_theme_dark(), web_theme_jama(). See Themes.

title: Quick Start
text:
Row styling
Build visual hierarchy with row types and styling:


Code
styled_data <- data.frame(
  label = c("Primary Endpoint", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Summary"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, 0.78),
  lower = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, 0.65),
  upper = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, 0.93),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "summary"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

tabviz(styled_data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold"
)












NoteRow types






Type
Behavior




"data"
Standard row with interval marker


"header"
Section header, no marker


"summary"
Summary row with diamond marker


"spacer"
Empty row for separation



Header and spacer rows should have NA for point, lower, and upper.

title: Quick Start
text:
Conditional styling
Style rows based on data values using formula expressions:


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_bold = ~ upper < 1,
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)






Or use pre-computed columns:


Code
direction_data <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    marker_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    )
  )

tabviz(direction_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  marker_color = "marker_color"
)

title: Quick Start
text:
Next steps



Topic
What you’ll learn




Columns
All 16+ column types


Row Styling
Headers, summaries, indentation, badges


Cell Styling
Conditional formatting for individual cells


Row Groups
Collapsible groups


Forest Plots
Advanced forest column options


Themes
Preset themes and customization

title: Visualization Styling
text: Every viz_* column — viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin — participates in the same color cascade. Markers, bars, boxes, and violins all resolve their fill (and sometimes their outline) by walking four layers from general to specific. Most of the time you only touch the layer that matches your intent and let the rest stay defaulted.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
The four layers
Each glyph’s color is decided by the most-specific layer that has a value:








Layer
Source
Wins when…




4. Per-row literal
marker_color = "col" or ~ formula on tabviz()
Value is non-NA for that row.


3. Row semantic class
row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted on tabviz()
Layer 4 is NA. Single-effect: replaces fill with theme color. Multi-effect: preserves per-effect fill, adds an outline in the theme color.


2. Per-effect literal
effect_*(color = "#hex") inside viz_*()
Layers 3–4 don’t apply. Sets the fill for that effect on every row.


1. Theme palette
theme.shapes.effect_colors[i]
Nothing else is set. Effects cycle through the palette by index.



Two things to internalize before the examples:

NA falls through. A marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "darkred", NA) colors the strict-significant rows red and lets every other row drop to whatever Layer 3 or below would have done.
Single vs. multi-effect matters at Layer 3. A semantic class on a single-effect viz repaints the glyph; on a multi-effect viz it adds an outline so the per-effect color identity (a key visual encoding) survives.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 1 — Theme palette
When you don’t say anything about color, viz cells pick from the active theme’s effect_colors palette. Single-effect viz takes the first color; multi-effect viz cycles by effect index.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  title = "Layer 1: theme palette",
  subtitle = "No color set anywhere — the marker takes the theme's first effect color"
)






Switching themes restyles automatically:


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "Same code, JAMA theme",
  subtitle = "The marker color tracks the active palette"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 2 — Per-effect color
Set color = "#hex" inside an effect_*() to nail down a single series. Useful when the encoding is intrinsic to the metric (“ITT is blue, per-protocol is green”) rather than to a row.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi",
                    label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",
                    label = "Per-protocol", color = "#16a34a")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  title = "Layer 2: per-effect colors",
  subtitle = "ITT and per-protocol get fixed identities across every row"
)






The same idea on viz_bar:


Code
sales <- data.frame(
  region = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),
  q1 = c(245, 189, 312, 98),
  q2 = c(278, 215, 345, 125),
  q3 = c(312, 242, 398, 156)
)

tabviz(sales, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("q1", label = "Q1", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_bar("q2", label = "Q2", color = "#22c55e"),
      effect_bar("q3", label = "Q3", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Sales ($K)"
    )
  ),
  title = "Per-effect colors on viz_bar",
  subtitle = "Each quarter holds its identity across regions"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 3 — Row semantic cascade
row_accent, row_emphasis, and row_muted already style row text (see Row Styling). They also reach into viz cells. The behavior depends on whether the viz column has one effect or several.

Single-effect: fill replacement
The marker fill is replaced with the theme color. Below, row_accent highlights strongly significant trials in the theme’s accent color, row_muted dims the non-significant ones.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  row_muted  = ~ pval >= 0.10,
  title = "Layer 3 (single-effect): fill replacement",
  subtitle = "Accented rows take the accent color; muted rows fade out"
)






The cascade is themable — the same code under web_theme_jama() uses JAMA’s accent color instead.


Multi-effect: outline augment
If the column has more than one effect, replacing the fill would destroy the per-effect color encoding. The cascade preserves the fill and adds an outline in the theme color, so each accented row gets a visible “this row is special” cue without losing its ITT/per-protocol identity.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi",
                    label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",
                    label = "Per-protocol", color = "#16a34a")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "Layer 3 (multi-effect): outline augment",
  subtitle = "Per-effect colors preserved; accented rows get an outline"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 4 — Per-row literal marker_color
For exact control — a categorical palette, a colorblind-safe encoding, or matching a brand color — pass a column or formula to marker_color. Returning NA falls through to the layers below.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "#b91c1c", NA),
  title = "Layer 4: per-row literal",
  subtitle = "p < 0.001 rows go dark red; the rest fall through to the theme"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Combined precedence
When multiple layers are active, the most-specific one wins per row. Below: the strictest-significance rows are pinned to dark red (Layer 4), the still-significant rows take the theme accent (Layer 3), and the rest fall through to the theme palette (Layer 1).


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent   = ~ pval < 0.05,                                    # Layer 3
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "#b91c1c", NA_character_), # Layer 4
  title = "All layers together",
  subtitle = "p < 0.001 → red (L4), 0.001–0.05 → accent (L3), > 0.05 → palette (L1)"
)

title: Visualization Styling
section: The cascade is uniform across viz types
text:
The cascade is uniform across viz types
Everything above works identically on viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin. A single example with a row_accent on each:

viz_bar


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("n"), header = "Enrollment", axis_label = "N enrolled")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ n > 1000,
  title = "row_accent on viz_bar",
  subtitle = "Bars for large trials take the accent color"
)








viz_violin


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_violin(effect_violin(data = "dist"),
               header = "Effect distribution",
               axis_label = "Posterior HR",
               show_quartiles = TRUE)
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "row_accent on viz_violin",
  subtitle = "Accented rows: the violin fill switches to the accent color"
)








viz_boxplot


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_boxplot(effect_boxplot(data = "dist"),
                header = "Effect distribution",
                axis_label = "Posterior HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "row_accent on viz_boxplot",
  subtitle = "Accented rows: the box fill switches to the accent color"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
When to reach for what

Stick with the theme when the viz is single-purpose and the row’s role isn’t categorical. The palette already does the right thing.
Use effect_*(color = ...) when the color identifies the metric (ITT vs. per-protocol, drug A vs. B). It belongs to the effect, not the row.
Use row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted when the color identifies the row (“this is the primary outcome”, “this is exploratory”). Themable, single-source: the row text and its viz both update.
Use marker_color (column or formula) when you need exact-pixel control — categorical row palettes, brand alignment, colorblind-safe encodings. Use NA to fall through to the cascade for rows you don’t want to override.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
See also

Row Styling — Text-side semantic classes that drive the same cascade
Styling Cascade — Higher-level overview of all four cascade layers across the package
Themes — Where effect_colors, accent, foreground, and muted live
Forest Plots, Bar Charts, Boxplots, Violin Plots — Per-viz feature reference

title: Boxplots
text: viz_boxplot() summarizes distributions with median, quartiles, and outliers. It accepts either raw arrays of values or pre-computed summary statistics.

title: Boxplots
text:
Mode 1 — From Array Data
When each row contains an array of values, boxplot statistics are computed automatically:


Code
set.seed(42)
boxplot_data <- data.frame(
  treatment = c("Control", "Low Dose", "Medium Dose", "High Dose"),
  response = I(list(
    rnorm(50, mean = 100, sd = 15),
    rnorm(50, mean = 110, sd = 18),
    rnorm(50, mean = 125, sd = 20),
    rnorm(50, mean = 140, sd = 25)
  )),
  n = c(50, 50, 50, 50)
)

tabviz(boxplot_data, label = "treatment",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "response", color = "#8b5cf6"),
      header = "Response Distribution",
      width = 250,
      show_outliers = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Dose-Response Distribution"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
Mode 2 — From Pre-computed Statistics
If you already have quartile statistics, map them directly:


Code
summary_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
  min = c(45, 52, 38, 60),
  q1 = c(62, 68, 55, 72),
  median = c(78, 82, 71, 85),
  q3 = c(89, 94, 86, 95),
  max = c(105, 112, 98, 108)
)

tabviz(summary_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(
        min = "min", q1 = "q1", median = "median", q3 = "q3", max = "max",
        color = "#22c55e"
      ),
      header = "Score Distribution",
      width = 220
    ),
    col_numeric("median", "Median")
  ),
  title = "Study Score Distributions"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
Multiple Effects
Compare distributions across conditions with multiple effect_boxplot() items:


Code
set.seed(123)
multi_box_data <- data.frame(
  timepoint = c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12"),
  placebo = I(list(
    rnorm(40, 50, 12), rnorm(40, 52, 13), rnorm(40, 51, 14), rnorm(40, 53, 12)
  )),
  treatment = I(list(
    rnorm(40, 50, 12), rnorm(40, 62, 14), rnorm(40, 70, 15), rnorm(40, 75, 13)
  ))
)

tabviz(multi_box_data, label = "timepoint",
  columns = list(
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "placebo", label = "Placebo", color = "#94a3b8"),
      effect_boxplot(data = "treatment", label = "Treatment", color = "#3b82f6"),
      header = "Score by Treatment Arm",
      width = 280,
      show_outliers = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Treatment Effect Over Time"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
viz_boxplot() Arguments



Argument
Default
Purpose




header
NULL
Column header text


width
150
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


axis_range
auto
Fixed axis range c(min, max)


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines


show_outliers
TRUE
Display outlier points


whisker_type
"iqr"
"iqr" (1.5×IQR) or "minmax"


axis_label
"Value"
Label text below the axis


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis

title: Boxplots
text:
effect_boxplot() Arguments







Argument
Purpose




data
Column containing array of values (mode 1)


min, q1, median, q3, max
Column names for pre-computed stats (mode 2)


outliers
Column containing outlier values (optional, mode 2)


label
Display label for legend


color
Box fill color


opacity
Box fill opacity (default 0.7). Was fill_opacity before 0.9.0.

title: Boxplots
text:
See Also

Visualization Styling — How row_accent / marker_color cascade into box fills
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, common features
Bar Charts — Single values with viz_bar()
Violin Plots — Full distributions with viz_violin()

title: Visualizations
text: tabviz embeds visualizations directly inside table columns. Four viz_*() column types cover the common patterns; each gets its own focused page.

title: Visualizations
text:
The Four viz_* Columns



Column
Use this when…
Guide




viz_forest()
You have point estimates with confidence intervals (meta-analyses, treatment effects)
Forest Plots


viz_bar()
You’re comparing single values across rows (counts, scores, percentages)
Bar Charts


viz_boxplot()
You care about median, spread, and outliers across distributions
Boxplots


viz_violin()
You need to see full distribution shapes (bimodality, skewness)
Violin Plots









Noteviz_* vs col_*



viz_*() columns are focal visualizations with their own axes and scales — designed for direct visual comparison across rows. The simpler col_*() family (col_bar(), col_sparkline()) is for inline cell content that fits in a cell without a dedicated axis.
If you want a quick weight indicator inside a row, reach for col_bar(). If you want a true comparable bar chart, reach for viz_bar().

title: Visualizations
text:
Common Features
All viz_*() columns share:

Axis configuration — axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label
Pan and zoom — mouse wheel, drag, double-click reset (per column)
Theming — colors adapt to the active theme; multi-effect series use the theme’s effect_colors palette
Marker-styling cascade — row-level row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted and per-row marker_color reach into viz cells. Single-effect viz: fill replacement. Multi-effect viz: outline augment. See Visualization Styling for the full 4-layer cascade with rendered examples across viz types.
Annotations — refline() for reference lines (and forest_annotation() on viz_forest). Use null_value as a shortcut for a single themed reference line.
Tooltips — opt in via web_interaction(tooltip_fields = ...)
Export — SVG / PNG / PDF via save_plot()







TipTheme Effect Colors



When you have multiple effects per row (multiple bar series, boxplot groups, or forest effects), colors come from the theme’s series_anchors input. Each anchor expands into a 7-field slot bundle (fill, stroke, fill_muted, etc.) at resolve time:
web_theme_cochrane() |>
  set_inputs(series_anchors = c("#2563eb", "#dc2626", "#16a34a"))
Individual effects can still override the theme via their own color parameter.

title: Visualizations
Visualizations
text:
Exporting Visualizations
p <- tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1)
))

save_plot(p, "figure.svg")                           # vector, no deps
save_plot(p, "figure.png", width = 1200, scale = 2)  # raster, needs rsvg
save_plot(p, "figure.pdf", width = 800)              # vector, needs rsvg
See Export for details.

text:

Boxplots — Distribution summaries with viz_boxplot()
