title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots display point estimates with confidence intervals — the standard visualization for meta-analyses and clinical trial results. Build them with the viz_forest() column inside tabviz(), or use the forest_plot() shortcut for quick one-liners.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Data Mapping
viz_forest() uses a column-mapping pattern: name the columns that hold your data, not the values themselves.


Code
data(effect_sizes)

effect_sizes |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ))







Required Mappings








Argument
What it is
Example




point
Point estimate column
Hazard ratio, odds ratio, mean difference


lower
Lower CI bound column
95% CI lower


upper
Upper CI bound column
95% CI upper





Handling Missing Values
tabviz uses NA strategically for structured layouts:

Header rows: row_type = "header" with NA for effect estimates
Spacer rows: row_type = "spacer" for visual separation
Missing estimates: data rows where the effect couldn’t be calculated



Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", row_type = "row_type", row_bold = "row_bold",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    )
  )

title: Forest Plots
text:
viz_forest() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




point / lower / upper
required (single-effect mode)
Column names for the point estimate and CI bounds


effects
NULL
List of effect_forest() items for multi-effect mode


header
NULL
Column header text


width
auto
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


null_value
NULL
Position of the dashed reference line (e.g. 1 for ratios, 0 for differences)


axis_range
auto
c(min, max) overriding the auto-calculated range


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines at tick positions


axis_label
NULL
X-axis label


annotations
NULL
List of refline() / forest_annotation() items


shared_axis
FALSE
Share axis range with sibling forest columns


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis

title: Forest Plots
text:
Scale and Null Value

Log Scale
Use for ratios (hazard ratio, odds ratio, risk ratio) — effects are multiplicative around null_value = 1:


Code
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
  ))












WarningLog Scale Requires Positive Values



All values in point, lower, and upper must be positive. Zero or negative values will error.




Linear Scale
Use for differences (mean difference, risk difference, SMD) — effects are additive around null_value = 0:


Code
diff_data <- data.frame(
  comparison = c("Treatment A", "Treatment B", "Treatment C"),
  mean_diff = c(-2.5, 1.3, -0.8),
  lower = c(-4.1, -0.2, -2.1),
  upper = c(-0.9, 2.8, 0.5)
)

tabviz(diff_data, label = "comparison", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "mean_diff", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "linear", null_value = 0,
             axis_label = "Mean Difference (95% CI)")
))

title: Forest Plots
text:
Multiple Effects
Display multiple effect estimates per row using effects = list(effect_forest(...), ...). Useful for comparing analyses or sensitivity scenarios:


Code
multi_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("PIONEER", "SUMMIT", "HORIZON"),
  n = c(2450, 1890, 3200),
  itt_or = c(0.72, 0.78, 0.65),
  itt_lo = c(0.58, 0.64, 0.52),
  itt_hi = c(0.89, 0.95, 0.81),
  pp_or = c(0.68, 0.73, 0.61),
  pp_lo = c(0.53, 0.58, 0.47),
  pp_hi = c(0.87, 0.92, 0.79)
)

tabviz(multi_data, label = "study", title = "Sensitivity Analysis Comparison",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_interval("itt_or", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", header = "Primary OR"),
    viz_forest(
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("itt_or", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
        effect_forest("pp_or", "pp_lo", "pp_hi", label = "Per-Protocol", color = "#16a34a")
      )
    )
  )
)







effect_forest() Arguments







Argument
Purpose




point
Column name for point estimates


lower
Column name for lower bounds


upper
Column name for upper bounds


label
Display label for legend


color
Override color for this effect


shape
Override shape: "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"





Side-by-Side Forest Columns
When comparing entirely different outcomes, separate viz_forest() columns are often clearer than overlaid effects:


Code
comparison_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Cardiovascular", "Mortality", "Hospitalization", "Composite"),
  na_hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.78, 0.76),
  na_lo = c(0.58, 0.71, 0.64, 0.65),
  na_hi = c(0.89, 1.02, 0.95, 0.89),
  eu_hr = c(0.68, 0.79, 0.82, 0.74),
  eu_lo = c(0.52, 0.62, 0.67, 0.61),
  eu_hi = c(0.89, 1.01, 1.00, 0.90)
)

tabviz(comparison_data, label = "study",
  title = "Regional Comparison: North America vs Europe",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "na_hr", lower = "na_lo", upper = "na_hi",
               header = "North America", scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
    viz_forest(point = "eu_hr", lower = "eu_lo", upper = "eu_hi",
               header = "Europe", scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180)
  )
)












TipSide-by-Side vs Multi-Effect




Side-by-side viz_forest() columns — comparing different outcomes or populations on potentially different scales.
Multi-effect overlays via effect_forest() — comparing the same outcome across analyses (sensitivity, ITT vs PP) on a single shared scale.





shared_axis
Set shared_axis = TRUE on multiple viz_forest() columns to lock them to a common axis range so the eye can compare across columns directly.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Marker Customization
Customize marker appearance based on data values using formula expressions on tabviz():


Code
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  head(6) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", title = "Marker Styling with Formulas",
    marker_shape = ~ ifelse(phase == "Phase 3", "circle", "square"),
    marker_color = ~ ifelse(significant, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8"),
    marker_opacity = ~ ifelse(n > 200, 1, 0.7),
    columns = list(
      col_text("phase", "Phase"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    )
  )














Argument
Type
Effect




marker_color
string / formula
CSS color for marker fill


marker_shape
string / formula
"square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"


marker_opacity
numeric / formula
Transparency (0–1)


marker_size
numeric / formula / "col"
Size multiplier (1 = default), or column name for proportional sizing



All marker arguments accept either a column name or a formula expression (~ ...). Pass marker_size = "col_name" to scale markers by a column (e.g. study weight in a meta-analysis).

title: Forest Plots
text:
Axis Control
Axis arguments live on viz_forest() itself — they’re per-column.

Axis Range


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_range = c(0.3, 2.0))
))








Custom Tick Values


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1,
             axis_range = c(0.3, 2.0),
             axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0))
))








Gridlines


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_gridlines = TRUE)
))

title: Forest Plots
text:
Reference Lines and Annotations
null_value draws a single dashed reference line. For more, pass annotations = list(...) of refline() / forest_annotation() items.


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", title = "With Custom Reference Line",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(0.8, label = "Threshold", style = "dashed", color = "#e11d48")
               ))
  )
)








Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", title = "Clinical Thresholds",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(0.5, label = "Strong", style = "solid", color = "#16a34a"),
                 refline(0.8, label = "Moderate", style = "dashed", color = "#f59e0b")
               ))
  )
)







refline() Arguments



Argument
Purpose




value
X-axis position


label
Optional label text


style
"solid", "dashed", "dotted"


color
Line color


width
Line width (default 1.5)


opacity
Line opacity 0–1 (default 0.6)



forest_annotation() adds richer annotations (regions, text callouts) — see the reference for the full surface.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Working with Meta-Analysis Results


Code
library(metafor)

res <- rma(yi = log_or, sei = se, data = studies, method = "REML")

forest_data <- studies |>
  mutate(
    or = exp(log_or),
    lower = exp(log_or - 1.96 * se),
    upper = exp(log_or + 1.96 * se)
  ) |>
  bind_rows(
    tibble(
      study = "Pooled Estimate",
      or = exp(res$b),
      lower = exp(res$ci.lb),
      upper = exp(res$ci.ub),
      row_type = "summary",
      row_bold = TRUE
    )
  )

tabviz(forest_data,
  label = "study", row_type = "row_type", row_bold = "row_bold",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Forest Plots
text:
See Also

Visualization Styling — How row_accent / marker_color cascade into marker colors
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, common features
Titles & Labels — Adding context to your plots
Row Styling — Headers, summaries, indentation
Themes — Visual styling

title: Styling Overview
text: tabviz styling is a four-layer cascade — each layer targets a more specific scope and overrides the layer above. This page is the map; follow the links for the details.

title: Styling Overview
text:
The Cascade
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Theme        — base colors, fonts, spacing         │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Row         — bold, color, background per row │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │  Cell      — per-column formatting       │  │  │
│  │  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐  │  │  │
│  │  │  │  Marker  — forest-plot points      │  │  │  │
│  │  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘  │  │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘  │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘








Layer
Where you set it
Use it for




Theme
theme = web_theme_*() on tabviz()
Overall look and feel


Row
row_bold, row_color, row_bg, … on tabviz()
Whole-row conditional formatting


Cell
bold, color, bg, … inside col_*()
Per-column overrides


Marker
marker_color, marker_shape, marker_size, marker_opacity on tabviz()
Forest-plot point appearance



More specific layers win: cell beats row, row beats theme, formula expressions beat everything.

title: Styling Overview
text:
1. Theme Level
Themes set the foundational palette, typography, spacing, and shapes.


Code
data(glp1_trials)
sample_data <- head(glp1_trials[glp1_trials$row_type == "data", ], 4)

tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
2. Row Level
Row styling applies formatting to entire rows based on data conditions.


Code
tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_bold = ~ upper < 1,
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
3. Cell Level
Cell styling targets individual columns. Use .x inside formulas to refer to the column’s own value.


Code
tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", decimals = 2,
                bold = ~ .x < 1,
                color = ~ ifelse(.x < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"))
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
4. Marker Level
Marker styling controls forest-plot point appearance: color, shape, size, opacity.


Code
tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  marker_shape = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "diamond", "square"),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
Formula Expressions
tabviz uses R’s formula syntax (~ expression) for conditional styling.

Row and marker formulas can reference any column in the data:
row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05
marker_color = ~ ifelse(group == "Treatment", "blue", "gray")
Cell formulas use .x for the current column’s value:
col_numeric("hr", "HR", bold = ~ .x < 1)
col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "red", "black"))

title: Styling Overview
text:
Next Steps

Row Styling — Row-level conditional formatting
Cell Styling — Per-column formatting
Themes — Built-in themes and customization
Fluent API — Pipe-based modifiers for complex cases

title: Columns
text: Columns are the unit of display in tabviz. You compose a table by passing a list of column specifications to tabviz(columns = ...). Each column helper (col_*(), viz_*()) picks a display type and exposes formatting knobs; the underlying web_col() constructor is there if you need something unusual.

title: Columns
text:
Universal arguments
Every col_*() helper accepts the same arguments for layout and styling. These behave identically regardless of column type.



Argument
Default
Purpose




Primary data (see per-helper table)
required
Which column(s) in data provide values


header
NULL (auto)
Column header text; defaults to the field name or a type-specific default


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


align
"left" (numeric types default to "right")
Body cell alignment: "left", "center", "right"


header_align
follows align
Header alignment; override to decouple


show_header
auto
TRUE/FALSE to force; NULL shows when header is non-empty


wrap
FALSE
Multi-line cell text. FALSE/0 = single line (ellipsis on overflow). TRUE/1 = up to 2 lines (1 extra). Integer n = up to n + 1 lines. Author \n is honoured first, then long segments word-wrap within the column width.


sortable
TRUE
Allow sort on header click


na_text
NULL
Text to display for NA/missing values (default is blank)


tooltip
NULL
Column whose values become per-cell hover text


formatter
NULL
R function function(x) ... applied to values; output rendered as text. See Custom formatters below.


bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent
NULL
Per-cell styling driven by another column — see Cell Styling









TipEverything routes through web_col()



The col_*() helpers are thin, opinionated wrappers over web_col(). When a helper doesn’t cover your case (custom type, non-standard option), call web_col() directly.

title: Columns
text:
Catalog at a glance








Helper
Use for
Type-specific arguments




col_text(field)
Plain text, categoricals
max_chars


col_label(field)
Row-identifier column
max_chars


col_numeric(field)
General numbers
decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_n(field)
Sample sizes
decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_percent(field)
Proportions / percentages
decimals, digits, multiply, symbol


col_currency(field)
Money
symbol, position, decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_pvalue(field)
P-values
stars, thresholds, format, digits, exp_threshold, abbrev_threshold


col_interval(point, lower, upper)
Point estimate + CI
decimals, separator, imprecise_threshold


col_events(events, n)
Events / N ratio
separator, show_pct, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_range(low, high)
Low – High range
separator, decimals, show_bar


col_date(field)
Dates
format


col_bar(field)
Inline magnitude bar (no axis)
max_value, show_label, color, scale


col_sparkline(field)
Trend charts
type, height, color


col_heatmap(field)
Color-scale values
palette, min_value, max_value, decimals, show_value, scale


col_progress(field)
Percentage progress bar
min_value, max_value, show_label, height, color


col_icon(field)
Icons / emoji
mapping, size, color


col_badge(field)
Status chips
variants, colors, size


col_stars(field)
Rating (1–N stars)
max_stars, color, empty_color, half_stars, min_value, max_value, size


col_img(field)
Inline images
height, max_width, fallback, shape


col_reference(field)
Truncated text + optional link
href_field, max_chars, show_icon


col_group(header, ...)
Group child columns under one header
—



For the four axis-backed visualization columns (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin), see Visualizations.

title: Columns
text:
Composing columns
A tabviz() call takes a list of column specs in display order. The special label argument produces an implicit leftmost identifier column; you can also declare it explicitly with col_label() if you want finer control.


Code
data(glp1_trials)

meta <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4)

tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  )
)

title: Columns
text:
Headers and widths
Headers default to the field name if omitted; pass a second positional argument (or named header) to override, and an empty string or show_header = FALSE to hide.
Widths are auto-sized by default. Pass a pixel value to fix a column.


Code
col_text("drug")                          # header: "drug", auto width
col_text("drug", "Drug")                  # header: "Drug"
col_text("drug", header = "", width = 80) # no header, 80px wide
col_text("drug", show_header = FALSE)     # header cell hidden

title: Columns
text:
Alignment
col_* helpers that render numeric content right-align by default (col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_range). Everything else left-aligns. Override with align; header alignment follows the body unless you set header_align explicitly.


Code
col_numeric("value", align = "left")                      # force body left
col_numeric("value", header_align = "center")             # center header only

title: Columns
text:
Numeric formatting
The numeric formatting vocabulary is shared across col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_heatmap, col_events, and col_interval. Use whichever helper fits your data — you get the same formatting primitives.







Argument
Meaning




decimals
Fixed number of decimal places. Mutually exclusive with digits.


digits
Significant figures. Mutually exclusive with decimals.


thousands_sep
Separator string (",", " ", ".", …) or FALSE to disable


abbreviate
TRUE to shorten large numbers: 1,234 → "1.2K", 2.5e6 → "2.5M"





Code
options_data <- data.frame(
  item  = c("Small", "Medium", "Large"),
  n     = c(125, 4520, 1250000),
  rate  = c(0.156, 0.892, 0.034),
  value = c(1.234, 12.34, 123.4)
)

tabviz(options_data, label = "item",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n",                              "Count"),
    col_n("n",        abbreviate = TRUE,    "Abbreviated", id = "n_abbr"),
    col_numeric("value", decimals = 2,      "2 decimals"),
    col_numeric("value", digits   = 3,      "3 sig figs",   id = "value_sigfig"),
    col_percent("rate",                     "Percent")
  )
)












NoteDecimals vs. digits



decimals = 2 always prints 2 places (0.10, 3.14, 125.00). digits = 3 prints 3 meaningful figures (0.156, 3.14, 125). Use digits when values span orders of magnitude.

title: Columns
text:
Handling missing data
Set na_text on any column to replace NA cells with custom text.


Code
col_numeric("response", na_text = "—")
col_pvalue("pvalue", na_text = "n/a")
col_events("events", "n", na_text = "—")


The default is a blank cell.

title: Columns
text:
Per-cell tooltips
Pass tooltip = "<col>" to any column helper to drive the cell’s hover-text from another column in the data. This overrides the default behavior of using the cell’s own value as the tooltip.


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("status", tooltip = "status_explanation"),  # custom hover text
    col_n("n", tooltip = "n_source")                      # e.g. "Per-protocol N"
  )
)


tooltip accepts a column name; the value at that cell becomes the rendered title attribute.

title: Columns
text:
Custom formatters
When the built-in formatting vocabulary doesn’t fit (decimals/digits/abbreviate/thousands_sep), pass a formatter function. The function is applied to the column’s values at render time and the output is treated as text — the column is rendered verbatim.


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  estimate = c(1.234, 0.85, 2.4),
  se       = c(0.12, 0.08, 0.31)
)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    web_col("estimate",
      formatter = function(x) sprintf("%.2f ± %.2f", x, data$se))
  )
)


The formatter receives the raw column vector; return a character vector of the same length. Use this for unit suffixes, conditional formatting, or composite values that don’t fit any single col_*() helper.






NoteWhen to reach for formatter



For routine formatting (decimals, sig figs, percentages, currencies, abbreviations), prefer the built-in arguments — they’re discoverable, declarative, and fast. Reach for formatter only when the desired output can’t be expressed via the built-in vocabulary.

title: Columns
Columns
text:
Column groups
col_group(header, ...) wraps a set of columns under a shared header row — useful for multiple outcomes, time points, or analyses.


Code
outcomes <- data.frame(
  study          = c("PARADIGM-HF", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR-Reduced"),
  primary_hr     = c(0.80, 0.74, 0.75),
  primary_lower  = c(0.73, 0.65, 0.65),
  primary_upper  = c(0.87, 0.85, 0.86),
  primary_p      = c(0.001, 0.001, 0.001),
  secondary_hr   = c(0.84, 0.70, 0.69),
  secondary_p    = c(0.01, 0.001, 0.001)
)

tabviz(outcomes, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "primary_hr", lower = "primary_lower",
               upper = "primary_upper", null_value = 1, scale = "log"),
    col_group("Primary",
      col_interval("primary_hr", "primary_lower", "primary_upper",
                   header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("primary_p", "P")
    ),
    col_group("Secondary",
      col_numeric("secondary_hr", "HR", decimals = 2),
      col_pvalue("secondary_p", "P")
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)

title: Columns
text:
Visualization columns
Four viz_*() columns render axis-backed plots inline: viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin. They share axis-control arguments (axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label, scale) and are documented separately:

Forest plots — point + CI with annotations
Bar charts — horizontal bars with an axis
Boxplots — quartiles, median, outliers
Violin plots — kernel-density distributions

Unlike col_bar, the viz columns share a consistent axis across rows.
All four viz columns honor the marker-styling cascade: row semantic classes (row_accent/row_emphasis/row_muted) and per-row marker_color from tabviz() reach into the viz cell automatically. Single-effect viz columns get a fill replacement; multi-effect columns get an outline that preserves per-effect color identity. See Row Styling → Cascading into viz cells for the full picture.

title: Columns
text:
Per-helper detail

Text and identifiers

col_text() and col_label()
Plain text with optional truncation. col_label() is the convenience helper for the row-identifier column; it also prettifies the field name into a Title Case header by default.


Code
col_text("drug")
col_text("notes", "Notes", max_chars = 50)      # truncate with ellipsis
col_text("description", wrap = TRUE, width = 220)   # wrap (up to 2 lines)
col_text("notes", wrap = 3, width = 200)            # up to 4 lines

col_label("study_id")                           # header auto: "Study Id"
col_label("study_id", header = "Study")          # explicit header





Numbers

col_numeric()
General-purpose numeric column with decimals / digits / thousands_sep / abbreviate.


Code
col_numeric("estimate")                          # 2 decimals
col_numeric("count", decimals = 0,
            thousands_sep = ",")                 # "12,345"
col_numeric("value", digits = 3)                 # 3 sig figs
col_numeric("population", abbreviate = TRUE)     # "1.2M"




col_n()
Convenience wrapper for integer counts: defaults to decimals = 0, thousands_sep = ",", header "N".


Code
col_n("n")
col_n("enrolled", header = "Enrolled", abbreviate = TRUE)




col_percent()
Formats proportions or percentages.








Arg
Default
Effect




multiply
TRUE
TRUE when input is [0, 1] (multiply by 100); set FALSE if your data is already on the 0–100 scale


symbol
TRUE
Append % to the formatted number





Code
col_percent("rate")                           # 0.05 → "5.0%"
col_percent("accuracy", multiply = FALSE)     # data is already 0–100
col_percent("share", symbol = FALSE)          # drop the "%"




col_currency()
Money with currency symbol prefix/suffix.








Arg
Default
Effect




symbol
"$"
Currency glyph (e.g. "€", "¥", "R$")


position
"prefix"
"suffix" for post-fixed currencies


thousands_sep
","
String or FALSE; European users often pass "."


abbreviate
FALSE
TRUE for "$1.2M"-style short form





Code
col_currency("price")
col_currency("amount", symbol = "€", thousands_sep = ".", position = "suffix")
col_currency("revenue", abbreviate = TRUE)    # "$1.2M"




col_pvalue()
P-values with smart formatting and optional significance stars.








Arg
Default
Effect




stars
FALSE
Append */**/*** based on thresholds


thresholds
c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001)
Star cutoffs


format
"auto"
"auto" / "scientific" / "decimal"


digits
2
Significant figures


exp_threshold
0.001
Below this, switch to scientific notation


abbrev_threshold
NULL
Below this, render as e.g. "<0.0001"





Code
col_pvalue("pval")
col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE)
col_pvalue("pval", abbrev_threshold = 0.0001)





Paired values

col_interval()
Renders a point estimate with its confidence interval: 0.85 (0.70, 1.03).








Arg
Default
Effect




point, lower, upper
required
Field names


decimals
2
Decimal places


separator
" "
Separator between point and CI block


imprecise_threshold
NULL
If upper/lower > threshold, render "—" instead





Code
col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
col_interval("or", "or_lo", "or_hi", decimals = 3, separator = ", ")
col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", imprecise_threshold = 10)




col_events()
Events / N ratio, optionally with percentage.


Code
col_events("events", "n")                           # "45/120"
col_events("events", "n", show_pct = TRUE)          # "45/120 (37.5%)"
col_events("events", "n", separator = " of ",
           abbreviate = TRUE)                       # "1.1K of 12K"




col_range()
Low – high range, optionally with an inline bar.


Code
col_range("age_min", "age_max", "Age")
col_range("ci_lower", "ci_upper", decimals = 1)
col_range("low_price", "high_price", show_bar = TRUE)





Dates

col_date()
Dates formatted with strftime-style tokens. R-side formatting, so the browser sees pre-formatted strings.


Code
col_date("enrollment_date", format = "%Y-%m-%d")
col_date("published",       format = "%b %Y")          # "Jan 2024"
col_date("visit",           format = "%A, %d %B %Y")    # full





Inline visuals

col_bar()
Small horizontal bar inside the cell — use for visual weight without a shared axis. For axis-backed bars across rows, reach for viz_bar().


Code
col_bar("weight")
col_bar("score", max_value = 100, color = "#2563eb")
col_bar("value", scale = "log")




col_sparkline()
Mini line/area/bar chart per row. Requires a list-column of numeric vectors.


Code
col_sparkline("trend")                          # default: line
col_sparkline("trend", type = "area")
col_sparkline("trend", type = "bar", height = 30, color = "#16a34a")








NotePreparing sparkline data



Create a list-column with mutate(trend = list(c(1, 2, 3))) or tibble(trend = I(list(...))). Vectors do not need to be the same length across rows.




col_heatmap()
Color-scale cell background with optional numeric overlay.


Code
col_heatmap("score")                                   # default palette
col_heatmap("score", palette = c("#fef3c7", "#92400e"),
            min_value = 0, max_value = 100)
col_heatmap("magnitude", scale = "log", show_value = FALSE)




col_progress()
Horizontal progress bar with numeric label.


Code
col_progress("completion", max_value = 100)
col_progress("share", max_value = 1, show_label = FALSE)
col_progress("fill", color = "#8b5cf6", height = 6)





Status and categories

col_badge()
Colored chips keyed to row values.







Arg
Effect




variants
Named list mapping values → semantic color ("success", "warning", "error", "info", "muted")


colors
Named list mapping values → literal CSS colors (overrides variants)


size
"base" or "sm"





Code
col_badge("status")                              # plain chips
col_badge("status", variants = list(
  Published = "success", Preprint = "warning", Retracted = "error"
))
col_badge("priority", colors = list(
  high = "#dc2626", medium = "#f59e0b", low = "#22c55e"
))




col_icon()
Icons, emoji, or unicode glyphs.


Code
col_icon("status_icon")                          # emoji already in data
col_icon("result", mapping = list(
  pass = "✔", fail = "✖", pending = "…"
))
col_icon("flag", size = "lg", color = "#16a34a")




col_stars()
Visual rating (filled/empty stars).


Code
col_stars("quality")                              # 1–5 default
col_stars("quality", max_stars = 10, half_stars = TRUE)
col_stars("score_0_100", min_value = 0, max_value = 100)   # remap to star count
col_stars("rating", size = "lg")                            # larger glyphs





Media and links

col_img()
Inline image cells. Images come from URL/path in the data column.


Code
col_img("logo_url")
col_img("avatar", shape = "circle", width = 40)
col_img("thumbnail", fallback = "No image")




col_reference()
Truncated text (e.g. citation) with optional clickable link and external-link icon.


Code
col_reference("citation")
col_reference("title", href_field = "doi_url",
              max_chars = 40)
col_reference("source", href_field = "url", show_icon = FALSE)





Grouping

col_group()
Wraps child columns under a shared header row. See Composing columns above.

title: Columns
text:
See also

Cell Styling — conditional formatting, formula pronouns
Row Styling — row-level bold/italic/color/bg/badge/icon
Visualizations — viz_*() axis-backed columns
web_col() reference — the low-level constructor

title: Interactivity
text: Every tabviz widget is fully interactive by default. This page is the authoritative reference for web_interaction() — the spec object that toggles which UI chrome appears and which user capabilities are enabled.

title: Interactivity
text:
Default Interactions
Every tabviz widget includes these interactions out of the box:







Interaction
Action




Sort
Click column headers to sort


Select
Click rows to select/deselect


Hover
Hover to highlight rows


Collapse
Click group headers to collapse/expand


Resize
Drag column borders to resize


Filter
Per-column filter popovers in headers


Reorder
Drag rows or columns to reorder


Edit
Double-click cells to edit — also works on existing title / subtitle / caption / footnote


Paint
Paint-brush button opens a popover for stamping emphasis / muted / accent onto rows or cells


Export
Download menu in header (hover to reveal)


Themes
Theme switcher in header





Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_jama(),
    footnote = "Try: click headers to sort, hover over rows, drag column borders"
  )

title: Interactivity
text:
The show_* vs enable_* Rule
web_interaction() uses two prefixes deliberately:

show_* controls static UI visibility — whether a piece of chrome appears at all (show_legend, show_group_counts). Nothing the user does at runtime brings it back.
enable_* controls user capabilities — whether a behavior is available (enable_sort, enable_filters, enable_edit, …). The associated UI is rendered when the capability is on, hidden when off.

When in doubt: render-or-not is show_; can-the-user-do-it is enable_.

title: Interactivity
text:
web_interaction() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




show_legend
TRUE
Show legend for multi-effect plots


show_group_counts
FALSE
Show (n) after each row-group label


tooltip_fields
NULL
Character vector of column names to show in marker hover tooltip


enable_sort
TRUE
Click headers to sort


enable_collapse
TRUE
Click group headers to collapse/expand


enable_select
TRUE
Click rows to select


enable_hover
TRUE
Highlight rows on hover


enable_resize
TRUE
Drag column borders to resize


enable_export
TRUE
Show download button in header


enable_filters
TRUE
Per-column filter popovers in column headers


enable_reorder_rows
TRUE
Drag to reorder rows (within a group)


enable_reorder_columns
TRUE
Drag to reorder columns (within a column group)


enable_edit
TRUE
Double-click inline editing for text/numeric/label cells


enable_themes
"default"
Theme menu: "default", NULL, or list of WebTheme

title: Interactivity
Interactivity
Interactivity
text:
Presets
tabviz ships three preset configurations.







Preset
Description




web_interaction()
Full interactivity (the default)


web_interaction_minimal()
Hover only — no sorting, selection, resize, export, filtering, reorder, edit


web_interaction_publication()
Fully static — every interaction off, no legend


web_interaction_full()
Explicit “everything on” — same as web_interaction() defaults




Minimal Interaction


Code
glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160)
    ),
    interaction = web_interaction_minimal(),
    theme = web_theme_jama()
  )








Publication Mode
web_interaction_publication() removes all interactivity — suitable for print, static documents, or screenshots.


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(...),
  interaction = web_interaction_publication()
)

title: Interactivity
text:
Theme Menu
The enable_themes argument controls the theme switcher. Three values are accepted:







Value
Effect




"default"
Show all themes returned by package_themes()


NULL
Hide the theme menu entirely


list(...)
Show only the listed WebTheme objects (named entries override displayed names)





Code
# Hide the theme menu
tabviz(data, interaction = web_interaction(enable_themes = NULL), ...)

# Curate a specific shortlist
tabviz(data, interaction = web_interaction(
  enable_themes = list(
    web_theme_jama(),
    web_theme_cochrane(),
    web_theme_cochrane()
  )
), ...)


selectable_themes() is a fluent sibling that does the same thing post-construction:


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study") |>
  set_theme("jama") |>
  selectable_themes(list(
    Classical = web_theme_jama(),
    Modern    = web_theme_cochrane()
  ))


The active theme is always auto-included in the menu so the user can revert.

title: Interactivity
text:
Pan & Zoom on Visualization Columns
Every viz column (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin) supports direct-manipulation pan and zoom on its x-axis:







Gesture
Action




Mouse wheel over the viz area
Zoom in/out, anchored on the cursor


Click and drag
Pan the axis


Double-click
Reset to the default domain



State is per column — two forest columns side-by-side can be inspected independently. It is session-only (refreshing the page resets everything); there is no R-side argument to persist a zoom level.
Exports are WYSIWYG: zooming or panning in the browser changes what save_plot() writes to SVG/PNG and what the in-widget download button produces. Confidence intervals that fall outside the zoomed window are rendered with boundary arrows; marks on bar/boxplot/violin columns are clipped to the visible region.
On log-scale forests (viz_forest(scale = "log")), gestures that would push the lower bound to zero or below are ignored.

title: Interactivity
text:
Row Selection
Selected rows are tracked internally and persist during sorting and filtering.


Code
sample_data <- data.frame(
  item = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta"),
  value = c(10, 25, 15, 30),
  change = c(0.05, -0.12, 0.08, 0.22)
)

tabviz(sample_data, label = "item",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("value", "Value"),
    col_percent("change", "Change")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  footnote = "Click rows to select (indicated by left border)"
)

title: Interactivity
text:
Paint Tool (v0.15+)
The paint-brush button on the widget toolbar opens a small popover where authors can stamp semantic classes onto rows or cells interactively. This is the in-widget equivalent of the R-side row_emphasis = <col> / row_muted = <col> / row_accent = <col> arguments, but applied by clicking rather than coded up front.
Flow:

Open the paint popover (brush icon in the top-right toolbar).
Pick a token: Emphasis, Muted, or Accent.
Flip the Scope switch between Row (whole row) and Cell (just one cell).
Click any row or cell to toggle the active flag on/off. The cursor turns into a crosshair while paint is active so you can spot where the next click lands.
Click the prominent Exit paint mode button at the top of the popover (or press Esc) when done. The brush icon stays lit in the toolbar’s accent color as long as paint is armed.

Persistence: painted flags merge into exportSpec, so save_plot() picks them up, and the flags round-trip through the “View source” button in the settings panel. You can also clear all paint with the “Clear all paint” button if you want to start over.
Painted flags coexist with R-side row_emphasis / etc. columns: the R column sets the baseline, paint overrides per row.

title: Interactivity
text:
Configure Columns (v0.18+)
Every col_* and viz_* column has a Configure… entry in its header context menu (right-click the column header, or use the column-menu chevron). The popover exposes the column’s relevant authoring arguments so you can tweak them without rebuilding the widget in R:

col_numeric — decimals, format.
col_interval — decimals, separator, imprecise threshold.
col_text — max chars, wrap.
viz_forest — scale, null value, axis label, and a full Axis section (range, tick count/values, gridlines, null tick, marker margin, symmetric).
viz_bar / viz_boxplot / viz_violin — full CRUD on effects (add / remove / reorder / rename / recolor / opacity / data field), plus the same Axis section. Boxplot can toggle between array-shaped and pre-computed stats data.

Double-clicking a viz column’s axis opens the configure popover pre-scrolled to the Axis section.
Changes merge into exportSpec and round-trip through the “View source” button — save_plot() picks them up. The interactive Axis theme tab was retired in v0.18 since axis values are now per-column.

title: Interactivity
text:
Title / Subtitle / Caption / Footnote Editing (v0.15+)
When enable_edit is on:

Double-click any existing title, subtitle, caption, or footnote to edit it inline (Enter to commit, Esc to cancel).
Add a missing label via the Basics tab in the settings panel — it exposes four text fields (Title / Subtitle / Caption / Footnote). Populating any of them makes the matching slot appear.

Edits merge into exportSpec.labels, so save_plot() reflects them.

title: Interactivity
Interactivity

Reference: Interaction Settings — Full API reference

Shiny Integration — Interactivity in Shiny apps

title: Guide Overview
text: This guide takes you from your first table to advanced styling, interactivity, and Shiny integration. Read in order, or jump to a chapter.

text:

title: Guide Overview
text:
Two entry points
tabviz() is the general-purpose constructor. forest_plot() is a thin convenience wrapper for the common case where you only need point + lower + upper.
# Full control
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("category"),
    col_numeric("value"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
    col_sparkline("trend")
  )
)

# Quick forest plot
forest_plot(data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)






TipWhen to reach for forest_plot()



Use forest_plot() when the table is “labels + a forest plot + maybe a CI string.” The moment you need extra columns, badges, or column groups, switch to tabviz().

title: Guide Overview
text:
Where else to look

Cheatsheet — one-page lookup for functions and arguments.
Concepts — architecture, mental models, and the styling cascade.
Reference — full API documentation.
Gallery — runnable examples by domain.
Troubleshooting — common issues and fixes.

title: Themes
text: A theme decides every visual choice in your table that isn’t data: surface colors, text tones, divider weights, spacing, fonts, marker fills. tabviz themes are designed so you can pick a preset, change one or two things, and never think about it again — or build a fully branded theme from a dozen inputs when you need to.

title: Themes
section: Three steps from “pick a preset” to “fully bespoke”
text:
Three steps from “pick a preset” to “fully bespoke”
Each step builds on the previous. Most users live at step 1.





flowchart LR
    A["1. Pick a preset"] --> B["2. Tweak inputs"]
    B --> C["3. Override component fields"]

    style A fill:#dbeafe
    style B fill:#bfdbfe
    style C fill:#93c5fd







Step 1 — theme = web_theme_lancet(). Done. Four presets cover the common publication and dashboard surfaces.
Step 2 — Swap a brand color, change density, switch to a serif font. The preset stays the foundation; you nudge a few inputs.
Step 3 — Reach into a specific component (e.g. the L1 row-group bar background) for surgical control. Rare; the preset cascade handles 95% of cases.

title: Themes
text:
Step 1: Pick a preset


Code
forest_plot(
  meta_data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  theme = web_theme_lancet()
)


Four presets ship in the package — each leans into a clear identity rather than trying to be everything:








Preset
Style
Designed for




web_theme_cochrane()
The package default. Cochrane teal + warm coral, Inter sans-serif, comfortable density
General purpose, systematic reviews


web_theme_lancet()
Lancet navy + refined old-gold + warm cream, Georgia serif, comfortable
Lancet-style editorial layouts


web_theme_jama()
Pure black-and-white, ultra-compact, strong dividers throughout, Arial
JAMA / print journal submissions


web_theme_dark()
Catppuccin Mocha-inspired dark canvas with pastel marker palette
Dark-mode viewing



Switch presets at any point with set_theme():


Code
my_table |> set_theme("jama")


The full registry is in package_themes().

Curating the in-widget switcher
The in-widget settings panel includes a theme dropdown that lets readers swap between presets without reloading. By default it shows all four package themes; pass enable_themes = list(...) to web_interaction() or selectable_themes() to curate the menu:


Code
# Flat list — no tabs, just a single column of choices.
selectable_themes(my_table, list(
  Cochrane = web_theme_cochrane(),
  Lancet   = web_theme_lancet()
))

# 2-level named list — each top-level key becomes a tab in the dropdown.
selectable_themes(my_table, list(
  Editorial = list(
    Cochrane = web_theme_cochrane(),
    Lancet   = web_theme_lancet(),
    JAMA     = web_theme_jama()
  ),
  Other = list(Dark = web_theme_dark())
))


The active theme is auto-included so readers can always revert. For categorized lists, if the active theme isn’t in any user-supplied category, a Current tab is prepended.

title: Themes
text:
Step 2: Tweak inputs
Every theme — including all four presets — is determined by a small set of customer-facing inputs. There are twelve of them:








Group
Input
What it controls




Chrome
neutral
5-step ramp, lightest → darkest. Drives surfaces, dividers, content



brand + brand_deep
Brand fill (markers, accents) and dark companion (text/strokes, bold-mode header band)



accent + accent_deep
Chrome accent for hover, selection, links



status_*
Positive / negative / warning / info


Data
series_anchors
Per-effect anchor colors (variable length). The first anchor also drives the pooled-effect diamond.


Type
font_body
Primary face for cells, headers, labels



font_display
Optional title face (defaults to body)



font_mono
Optional monospace face (e.g. for IDs)



Build a custom theme by overriding any of these:


Code
my_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "my-brand",
  inputs = list(
    brand          = "#1F3A5F",   # navy
    accent         = "#B08938",   # warm gold
    series_anchors = c("#1F3A5F", "#B08938", "#6B8E7F"),
    font_body      = "Charter, Georgia, serif"
  )
)


web_theme() starts from web_theme_cochrane() unless you pass a different base_theme. Anything you don’t set inherits from the base.
The deep companions mirror their primaries by default:

brand_deep defaults to brand (override when you want a darker stroke than fill, or a richer bold-mode header band).
accent_deep defaults to accent.
font_display defaults to font_body.

The pooled-effect diamond reads from the first series slot bundle (theme@series[[1]]). Override series_anchors[1] to recolor it; reach into theme@series[[1]]@stroke directly for a journal-specific outline.

What brand_deep actually drives
brand_deep is the dark identity tone — meant to land on text and rules where you want the brand to read as serious, not decorative. The resolver threads it into:

Plot title color (text@title@fg)
Forest-axis label and tick-label colors (plot@axis_label@fg, plot@tick_label@fg)
Cell-hairline tint — divider@subtle is neutral[3] mixed with 8% brand_deep, so cell borders pick up a faint identity color without muddying out
Bold-mode header band (header@bold@bg) and column-group bold band
Bold-mode header rule (header@bold@rule) via the new divider@strong_on_dark helper — a 40%-deep / 60%-inverse mix that contrasts against the band instead of disappearing into it
L1 group bar — but only when the active variant is header_style = "bold". Under light-mode the L1 bar stays on accent.tint_subtle (chrome/data wall: brand only “leaks” into chrome when you’ve chosen brand-forward chrome).

To pin any of these, set the field directly via set_theme_field() and your value survives re-resolution. The interactive panel surfaces the same override semantics as a per-field reset icon.


L1 group header and banding
When theme@row@banding cycles at the L1 level (the default for every preset — banding = "group" with one nesting level), the banding cycle already paints alternating fills across the L1 group rows. The renderer detects this and drops the explicit theme@row_group@L1@bg paint on the affected rows so the banded color reads as continuous and the group-bar doesn’t double-paint on top.
If you want a custom L1 bar paired with a banded background, switch banding modes (e.g. set_theme_field(c("row", "banding"), "row") or "none") so the L1 paint isn’t subsumed.
At deeper banding levels (e.g. banding = "group-2" with nested groups), the L1 bar’s explicit fill is preserved unchanged — only the banded level loses its explicit fill.


Semantic tokens — naming visual treatments
Tabviz ships with six semantic tokens that name visual treatments you can apply to a row or cell. Each token is a RowSemantic bundle on theme.row: bg, fg, border, marker_fill, font_weight, font_style. The renderer picks one token per row/cell and paints its bundle.







Token
Default treatment




mute
Lighter color, reduced prominence


bold
Just a weight bump — no color change


accent
Bold + accent-color text and marker (most common)


highlight
Bold + a pale “marker” background (defaults to a derived theme.semantic.highlight)


fill
Bold + a strong row fill (defaults to a derived theme.semantic.fill); fg auto-contrasts


emphasis
Legacy: bold + primary text and marker (kept for back-compat)



Two paths to apply a token to a row:

Data column — pass a logical column to tabviz():


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")),
  row_accent = "is_primary",      # rows where is_primary == TRUE → accent token
  row_fill   = "is_overall"       # rows where is_overall == TRUE → fill token
)


Painter (interactive) — click the paintbrush icon, pick a chip (mute / bold / accent / highlight / fill), then click rows or cells to stamp the token on. Hover before clicking shows a semi-transparent preview of the would-be visual. The painter defaults to accent + row-scope on first open. Toggle Row/Cell scope from the popover.


Customizing token visuals
Two ways to override:

Tier-2 named colors for the highlight + fill backgrounds:


Code
set_theme_field(t, c("semantic", "highlight"), "#FFE58F")  # custom marker yellow
set_theme_field(t, c("semantic", "fill"),      "#1F3A5F")  # custom fill navy


Or interactively: Settings panel → Tokens tab → Token colors.
Per-bundle override for any field on any token:


Code
set_theme_field(t, c("row", "fill", "fg"),          "#FFFFFF")  # force white text on fill rows
set_theme_field(t, c("row", "highlight", "border"), "#C8553D")  # red border on highlight rows


Or interactively: Settings panel → Tokens tab → expand a token → edit any field. All bundle fields are override-aware (reset reverts to the resolved default).




Variants — three knobs every table can flip
Variants are per-table choices that don’t change the theme’s identity. They’re orthogonal to inputs and freely composable:


Code
web_theme(
  inputs = list(brand = "#1F3A5F"),
  variants = list(
    density            = "comfortable",   # compact | comfortable | spacious
    header_style       = "bold",          # light  | bold
    first_column_style = "bold"           # default | bold (Excel-style)
  )
)



density drives every spacing token (row height, padding, gaps). The three presets cover almost every screen and print scenario.
header_style picks between a light header on the muted surface and a bold header on brand_deep with inverse text. Both options are always available on every theme — you flip the variant per table.
first_column_style gives the row-label column an Excel-style emphasized treatment (background tint + heavier weight). Useful when your first column is genuinely a label, not data.

You can also set variants directly on the tabviz() call as overrides (see tabviz reference).

title: Themes
section: How it cascades under the hood
text:
How it cascades under the hood
A theme has three tiers. You only ever interact with Tier 1; the other tiers are computed.





flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Tier 1 — Inputs (you edit these)"]
      neutral & brand & accent & series & fonts & density
    end

    subgraph T2C["Tier 2 — Chrome roles (derived)"]
      surface[surface.base / muted / raised]
      content[content.primary / secondary / muted / inverse]
      divider[divider.subtle / strong]
      tints[accent.default / muted / tint.subtle / tint.medium]
    end

    subgraph T2D["Tier 2 — Data roles (derived)"]
      slots[per-series SlotBundle: fill, stroke, fill.muted, ...]
    end

    subgraph T3["Tier 3 — Component bindings"]
      header & row & cell & rowGroup & plot & marks
    end

    T1 --> T2C
    T1 --> T2D
    T2C --> T3
    T2D --> T3

    style T1 fill:#dbeafe
    style T2C fill:#dcfce7
    style T2D fill:#fef3c7
    style T3 fill:#f3e8ff






Two cascades, sharing only Tier 1. Chrome derivation reads only chrome inputs (neutrals + brand + accent). Data derivation reads only data inputs (series anchors + status). Series anchors never affect surface tones; brand colors never bleed into row-group divider tints. This isolation makes themes predictable: changing series_anchors cannot accidentally shift the column-header background.
OKLCH derivation. All blends, lightenings, and darkenings happen in OKLCH (perceptually uniform). The result: tints stay readable across the brightness range, and high-chroma brands don’t mud out when blended with surfaces.
Slot bundles. Each series anchor expands into a 7-field bundle: fill, stroke, fill_muted, stroke_muted, fill_emphasis, stroke_emphasis, text_fg. The renderer picks the right field based on row state (default / muted / emphasized) without you having to define every state by hand. Override individual fields if you need to.
If you want to inspect a resolved theme:


Code
t <- web_theme_lancet()
t@surface@base       # "#FDFCFB"
t@content@primary    # "#1E3A5F"
t@accent@tint_subtle # tinted accent for L1 group bar
t@series[[1]]@fill   # first series anchor
t@series[[1]]@stroke # auto-darkened companion
t@row@hover@bg       # accent.muted blend

title: Themes
Themes
text:
Going deeper: spacing and typography

Density preset values



Token
compact
comfortable
spacious




row_height
20
24
30


header_height
26
32
40


padding
8
12
16


axis_gap
8
12
16


cell_padding_x
8
10
14


column_group_padding
6
8
12


title_subtitle_gap
10
13
18


bottom_margin
12
16
22


indent_per_level
14
16
20



set_spacing() overrides any of these per-token; the rest fall back to the active density preset. Read Density for how spacing tokens flow into the renderer.


Typography roles
A theme exposes eight named text roles:



Role
Used for




title
Plot title (display family, large, semibold)


subtitle
Subtitle (body family, medium)


caption
Italic caption above the plot


footnote
Footnote below the plot


body
Default body text (cells, headers when not bold)


cell
Data cell text (defaults to body)


label
Axis labels


tick
Axis tick labels (smaller)



Edit individual fields with set_theme_field():


Code
web_theme_cochrane() |>
  set_theme_field(c("text", "title", "weight"), 700) |>
  set_theme_field(c("text", "title", "italic"), TRUE)

title: Themes
section: The 9 presets — when to use which
text:
The 9 presets — when to use which


Code
# Black & white journal style — JAMA submissions, dense print
forest_plot(meta_data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study", theme = web_theme_jama())

# Lancet navy with serif typography — Lancet submissions
forest_plot(meta_data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study", theme = web_theme_lancet())

# Modern dashboard — vibrant, spacious, screen-first
forest_plot(meta_data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study", theme = web_theme_cochrane())

# Slide deck — oversized markers, thick lines, large type
forest_plot(meta_data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study", theme = web_theme_cochrane() |> set_variants(density = "spacious"))

# Dark mode — Catppuccin-inspired
forest_plot(meta_data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study", theme = web_theme_dark())

title: Themes
text:
Editing themes interactively
Every rendered table carries a settings gear. The in-widget panel exposes the v2 theme model under five tabs:







Tab
Edits




Theme
Tier 1 inputs — brand, accent, status, series anchors, fonts


Layout
Variants (density / header style / first column style), banding, container border


Spacing
Per-token overrides on top of the density preset (collapsed Advanced section)


Marks
Series slot bundles (anchor + expandable 7-field editor), summary slot, plot mark sizes


Text
Eight text-role bundles + L1/L2/L3 row-group accordions



The “View source” button exports your edits as v2 R code (web_theme(inputs = list(...)) or a chain of set_*() calls), so panel exploration translates back into reproducible theme construction.

title: Themes
section: Saving a custom theme as a function
text:
Saving a custom theme as a function
For team-wide use, wrap your custom theme in a named function:


Code
web_theme_acme <- function() {
  web_theme(
    name = "acme",
    inputs = list(
      neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFAFA", "#EEEEEE", "#888888", "#222222"),
      brand          = "#FF5500",
      brand_deep     = "#CC3300",
      accent         = "#0066CC",
      series_anchors = c("#FF5500", "#0066CC", "#00AA66", "#993399", "#666666"),
      font_body      = "Inter, system-ui, sans-serif",
      font_display   = "'Inter Display', Inter, system-ui, sans-serif"
    ),
    variants = list(density = "comfortable", header_style = "bold")
  )
}

# Now share it across the team
forest_plot(data, ..., theme = web_theme_acme())


Pass to set_theme() either by string (when registered as a preset) or by handing the constructed WebTheme object directly:


Code
my_table |> set_theme(web_theme_acme())

title: Themes
text:
Reference summary

web_theme() — custom-theme constructor (inputs + variants + base).
web_theme_*() — 9 preset constructors.
package_themes() — named list of all preset constructors.
set_theme(spec, theme) — switch a WebSpec to a different theme.
set_inputs(theme, ...) — update Tier 1 inputs.
set_variants(theme, ...) — update per-table variants.
set_spacing(theme, ...) — per-token spacing overrides.
set_theme_field(theme, path, value) — generic deep setter.
selectable_themes(spec, themes) — curate the in-widget theme switcher.

For complete argument lists and class definitions see the Themes reference.

title: Dashboard Examples
text: Information-dense dashboards built from tabviz() column primitives — heatmaps, progress bars, sparklines, badges, currency, and the occasional in-line forest plot. Each example targets a different domain (product, ops, climate, sports) but uses the same data-frame-plus-columns API.

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Product Metrics Dashboard
Track product KPIs with sparklines, bars, stars, currency, and trend indicators:


Code
product_metrics <- data.frame(
  product = c("Widget Pro", "Widget Lite", "Widget Plus", "Widget Max", "Widget Mini"),
  revenue = c(2450000, 1890000, 3200000, 1560000, 980000),
  growth = c(0.12, -0.05, 0.28, 0.08, 0.15),
  margin = c(0.35, 0.42, 0.28, 0.38, 0.45),
  units_sold = c(12500, 9800, 18200, 7800, 15600),
  satisfaction = c(4.2, 3.8, 4.5, 4.1, 4.3),
  trend = I(list(
    c(180, 195, 210, 205, 225, 240, 235, 250, 260, 275, 290, 305),
    c(220, 215, 205, 210, 200, 195, 190, 185, 188, 182, 180, 178),
    c(200, 220, 240, 260, 290, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440),
    c(120, 125, 130, 128, 135, 140, 138, 145, 150, 148, 155, 160),
    c(60, 68, 75, 82, 88, 95, 100, 108, 115, 122, 128, 135)
  ))
)

tabviz(product_metrics, label = "product",
  columns = list(
    col_currency("revenue", "Revenue", decimals = 0),
    col_percent("growth", "YoY"),
    col_percent("margin", "Margin"),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("units_sold"),
      header = "Units Sold",
      axis_range = c(0, 20000)
    ),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12M Trend"),
    col_stars("satisfaction", "Rating", max_stars = 5)
  ),
  row_color = ~ ifelse(growth < 0, "#dc2626", "#64748b"),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Product Performance Dashboard"
)

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
A/B Test Results
Compare conversion rates across experiment variants with confidence intervals and winner badges:


Code
ab_test_results <- data.frame(
  variant = c("Control", "Variant A", "Variant B", "Variant C"),
  visitors = c(50000, 48500, 51200, 47800),
  conversions = c(2500, 2910, 2816, 2629),
  rate = c(0.050, 0.060, 0.055, 0.055),
  lift = c(0, 0.20, 0.10, 0.10),
  lower = c(NA, 0.08, -0.02, -0.03),
  upper = c(NA, 0.32, 0.22, 0.23),
  pvalue = c(NA, 0.002, 0.12, 0.15),
  verdict = c("Baseline", "Winner", "Inconclusive", "Inconclusive"),
  row_type = c("summary", "data", "data", "data"),
  row_bold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(ab_test_results, label = "variant",
  columns = list(
    col_n("visitors", "Visitors", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_n("conversions", "Conv"),
    col_percent("rate", "CVR"),
    viz_forest(
      point = "lift", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
      null_value = 0,
      axis_label = "Lift vs Control"
    ),
    col_percent("lift", "Lift"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE),
    col_badge("verdict", "Verdict",
      variants = list(
        Baseline = "info",
        Winner = "success",
        Inconclusive = "warning"
      ))
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(is.na(lower), "#64748b",
                          ifelse(lower > 0, "#16a34a", "#64748b")),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Homepage Redesign A/B Test"
)

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Airline Performance
Airline delay metrics with heatmaps for on-time percentage and progress bars for satisfaction:


Code
data(airline_delays)

airline_summary <- airline_delays |>
  filter(month == "Apr") |>
  select(carrier, carrier_type, on_time_pct, delay_vs_avg, satisfaction, flights, trend)

tabviz(
  airline_summary,
  label = "carrier",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("carrier_type", "Type",
      variants = list(
        Legacy = "info",
        `Low-Cost` = "success",
        `Ultra Low-Cost` = "warning"
      )),
    col_n("flights", "Flights", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_heatmap("on_time_pct", "On-Time %",
      palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
      min_value = 0.65, max_value = 0.95,
      decimals = 2, show_value = TRUE),
    col_progress("satisfaction", "Satisfaction", max_value = 5),
    col_numeric("delay_vs_avg", "Delay vs Avg", decimals = 1),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12-Month Trend")
  ),
  row_bg = ~ case_when(
    on_time_pct > 0.85 ~ "#dcfce7",
    on_time_pct < 0.75 ~ "#fee2e2",
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Airline Performance Dashboard"
)

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Executive Summary
A visually rich C-suite dashboard using progress bars, heatmaps, sparklines, and badges:


Code
exec_data <- data.frame(
  metric = c("Revenue", "Profit Margin", "Customer Count", "Retention Rate",
             "Net Promoter Score", "Employee Satisfaction"),
  current_val = c(142000000, 0.197, 45200, 0.92, 72, 4.1),
  target_val = c(150000000, 0.200, 50000, 0.95, 75, 4.5),
  pct_to_target = c(0.947, 0.985, 0.904, 0.968, 0.960, 0.911),
  health = c(88, 92, 78, 95, 85, 72),
  status = c("On Track", "On Track", "Behind", "On Track", "On Track", "At Risk"),
  trend = I(list(
    c(120, 125, 130, 128, 135, 138, 140, 142),
    c(0.18, 0.185, 0.19, 0.188, 0.192, 0.195, 0.196, 0.197),
    c(38, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 44.5, 45.2),
    c(0.88, 0.89, 0.90, 0.91, 0.91, 0.92, 0.92, 0.92),
    c(65, 67, 68, 70, 71, 72, 72, 72),
    c(3.8, 3.9, 3.9, 4.0, 4.0, 4.1, 4.1, 4.1)
  ))
)

tabviz(exec_data,
  label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("status", "Status",
      variants = list(
        `On Track` = "success",
        Behind = "warning",
        `At Risk` = "error"
      )),
    col_progress("pct_to_target", "Progress to Target", max_value = 1),
    col_heatmap("health", "Health",
      palette = c("#fef3c7", "#dcfce7"),
      min_value = 60, max_value = 100, decimals = 0),
    col_sparkline("trend", "8-Quarter Trend")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |> set_variants(density = "spacious"),
  title = "Q4 Executive Summary"
)

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Financial Benchmarks
A SaaS peer-group benchmark: revenue in millions USD, YoY growth, gross margin, quartile ranking, and a quarterly ARR trend sparkline. Row background is colored by quartile.


Code
set.seed(2026)
saas_peers <- data.frame(
  company = c("Aurora Cloud", "Beacon Labs", "Cirrus IO", "Delta Forge",
              "Ember Systems", "Fathom AI", "Helios Data", "Ion Works",
              "Juno Metrics", "Kestrel Security"),
  revenue_m = c(842, 612, 1204, 318, 186, 2450, 945, 428, 276, 1567),
  yoy_growth = c(0.28, 0.42, 0.18, 0.65, 0.88, 0.22, 0.35, 0.52, 0.71, 0.15),
  margin = c(0.74, 0.68, 0.81, 0.59, 0.52, 0.85, 0.77, 0.63, 0.55, 0.79),
  arr_trend = I(lapply(1:10, function(i) {
    base <- runif(1, 80, 220)
    cumsum(c(base, rnorm(11, mean = base * 0.08, sd = base * 0.04)))
  })),
  quartile = c("Q2", "Q1", "Q2", "Q1", "Q1", "Q3", "Q2", "Q1", "Q1", "Q3")

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Academic / Education
Meta-analysis of test-score interventions grouped by grade band. Effect sizes are Cohen’s d with 95% CIs; forest plot uses null_value = 0 with a linear scale.


Code
set.seed(7)
n_int <- 14
interventions <- data.frame(
  intervention = c(
    "Phonics Tutoring", "Small-Group Reading", "1:1 Math Coach",
    "Peer Tutoring", "Summer Bridge", "After-School Math",
    "Writing Workshop", "STEM Lab Rotation", "Flipped Classroom",
    "Project-Based Learning", "Socratic Seminars", "AP Prep Bootcamp",
    "Career Mentorship", "Digital Literacy"
  ),
  grade_level = c("K-5", "K-5", "K-5", "K-5",
                  "6-8", "6-8", "6-8", "6-8", "6-8",
                  "9-12", "9-12", "9-12", "9-12", "9-12"),
  n = c(842, 1204, 416, 988, 612, 524, 368, 892, 1056, 742, 488, 934, 612, 1120),
  effect_size = c(0.42, 0.58, 0.71, 0.33, 0.28, 0.12, 0.45, 0.38,
                  0.52, 0.22, 0.61, 0.35, 0.48, -0.04),
  ci_lo = c(0.28, 0.41, 0.52, 0.18, 0.11, -0.05, 0.22, 0.22,
            0.38, 0.05, 0.42, 0.19, 0.28, -0.20),
  ci_hi = c(0.56, 0.75, 0.90, 0.48, 0.45, 0.29, 0.68, 0.54,
            0.66, 0.39, 0.80, 0.51, 0.68, 0.12)
) |>
  mutate(
    status = case_when(
      ci_lo > 0.2 ~ "Pass",
      ci_hi < 0.1 ~ "Fail",
      TRUE ~ "Improvement"
    )
  )

tabviz(interventions,
  label = "intervention",
  group = "grade_level",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "effect_size", lower = "ci_lo", upper = "ci_hi",
               scale = "linear", null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "Effect size (Cohen's d)"),
    col_interval("effect_size", "ci_lo", "ci_hi", header = "d (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_badge = "status",
  theme = web_theme_lancet(),
  title = "Test-Score Interventions by Grade Band",
  subtitle = "Row groups: K-5 / 6-8 / 9-12. Badge shows pre-registered pass criterion (d > 0.2, CI excludes 0).",
  footnote = "Synthetic data modeled on typical education meta-analysis effect sizes."
)

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Operations / QC
Manufacturing-line process monitoring: capacity utilization bars, yield sparklines, a defect-rate heatmap, and a status badge. row_bg flags critical lines.


Code
set.seed(99)
qc_lines <- data.frame(
  line = c("Line-01", "Line-02", "Line-03", "Line-04", "Line-05",
           "Line-06", "Line-07", "Line-08", "Line-09"),
  defect_rate = c(0.018, 0.042, 0.009, 0.068, 0.021, 0.015, 0.055, 0.033, 0.012),
  target = rep(0.025, 9),
  capacity_used = c(0.82, 0.74, 0.91, 0.58, 0.88, 0.79, 0.45, 0.85, 0.93),
  yield_trend = I(lapply(1:9, function(i) {
    base <- runif(1, 0.88, 0.98)
    pmin(1, pmax(0.75, base + cumsum(rnorm(24, 0, 0.01))))
  })),
  status = c("In control", "Warning", "In control", "Critical", "In control",
             "In control", "Critical", "Warning", "In control")
)

tabviz(qc_lines, label = "line",
  columns = list(
    col_heatmap("defect_rate", "Defect Rate",
      palette = c("#dcfce7", "#fee2e2"),
      min_value = 0, max_value = 0.08, decimals = 3),
    col_numeric("target", "Target", decimals = 3),
    col_bar("capacity_used", "Capacity", max_value = 1),
    col_sparkline("yield_trend", "Yield (24h)"),
    col_badge("status", "Status",
      variants = list(
        `In control` = "success",
        Warning = "warning",
        Critical = "error"
      ))
  ),
  row_bg = ~ case_when(
    status == "Critical" ~ "#fef2f2",
    status == "Warning" ~ "#fffbeb",
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Production-Line QC Dashboard",
  subtitle = "Defect rate vs target, capacity utilization, and 24-hour yield trend"
)

title: Dashboard Examples
text:
Climate Science: Regional Temperature Anomalies
Environmental data example using the climate_temps dataset — the same interval-estimate structure used in clinical forest plots maps cleanly onto temperature anomalies vs. a pre-industrial baseline.


Code
data(climate_temps)

tabviz(
  climate_temps,
  label = "region",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_text("period", "Period"),
    col_text("certainty", "Confidence"),
    col_interval("anomaly", "lower", "upper", header = "Anomaly °C (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "anomaly", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 0, axis_label = "Temperature Anomaly (°C vs. pre-industrial)")
  ),
  marker_shape = ~ ifelse(certainty == "High", "circle", "square"),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(anomaly > 1.5, "#dc2626", ifelse(anomaly > 1.0, "#f59e0b", "#3b82f6")),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE),
  title = "Regional Temperature Anomalies",
  subtitle = "Grouped by geographic category, shaped by confidence level",
  caption = "Red = significant warming, Blue = cooling or below baseline"
)

title: Clinical Examples
text: Publication-style meta-analyses and trial figures. Each example pairs a viz_forest() column with a textual col_interval() summary, then swaps the theme to match a target journal.

title: Clinical Examples
text:
JAMA-Style Forest Plot
Classic medical-journal style with clean typography:


Code
data(glp1_trials)

tabviz(
  glp1_trials,
  label = "study",
  group = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
  subtitle = "Major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)"
)












TipTheme Variations



The same data structure works with any theme. Try web_theme_lancet(), web_theme_cochrane(), or web_theme_cochrane() for different journal styles. See Feature Showcases for a visual comparison of all 9 built-in themes.
